source.title: Hotfix: prevent burst starvation under telemetry lag
source.author: Jordan Lee
source.description: Context: During Dedicated burst beta we observed brief "denial storms" where one tenant would get disproportionately denied burst admissions for ~30–90s after a telemetry delay event. This manifested as burst capacity being available (per fleet dashboards) but the admission controller continuing to deny with reason=POOL_SATURATED / FAIRNESS_THROTTLE for that tenant, while other tenants recovered faster. Root cause: the fairness + pool-availability calculation in the admission controller was using a raw (unsmoothed) free-capacity signal derived from fleet telemetry. When telemetry arrived late/out-of-order, the computed free_capacity could transiently drop to ~0 and stay pinned until the next sample; the token-bucket refill path treated that as hard saturation and stopped refilling, effectively starving tenants that had recently spent tokens. Fix: (1) add EWMA smoothing and monotonic guards for free-capacity + utilization signals, (2) add telemetry staleness detection (age > telemetry_max_age_ms) and switch to a conservative "stale mode" that prevents starvation by allowing minimal refill and limiting denial amplification, while still respecting per-tenant burst caps and global fleet safety caps, and (3) add explicit guard conditions to avoid negative/implausible deltas causing refill=0 for an entire window. This PR is intentionally scoped as a hotfix: no API shape changes, no new config required for existing tenants; defaults are safe and feature-flagged (burst.telemetry_smoothing_enabled) for rollback. Test plan: unit tests for smoothing + stale mode; simulation test that replays out-of-order telemetry and verifies no tenant sees >X consecutive denials solely due to telemetry staleness; staging canary with synthetic telemetry lag injection. Rollout: enable flag for internal dogfood tenants first, then beta allowlist (us-east/H100) after 24h with no SLO regression; keep kill-switch available via config bundle.
commits: admission-controller: add EWMA smoother for pool_free_capacity and clamp invalid samples
admission-controller: introduce telemetry staleness mode and minimal refill guard
policy-engine: plumb telemetry_age_ms into decision context (internal only)
tests: add replay test for out-of-order telemetry + anti-starvation assertion
metrics: emit burst_telemetry_stale_total and burst_free_capacity_smoothed gauge
config: add burst.telemetry_smoothing_enabled feature flag (default on for beta env)
conversation: Jordan Lee (author): We saw repeated FAIRNESS_THROTTLE denials on Aurora tenant right after a telemetry lag spike in eu-west staging. Traced to free_capacity computed from delayed samples going to 0 and pinning refill. This hotfix adds smoothing + stale-mode to avoid starvation. Please focus review on correctness/guardrails; perf impact is minimal (single EWMA per pool).
Keiko Yamamoto (reviewer): In stale mode, are we risking over-admitting and hurting neighbors? What stops a tenant from getting a "free pass" when telemetry is stale?

Jordan Lee (author): Stale mode still enforces: (a) per-tenant burst_gpu_limit, (b) per-tenant max_burst_ratio, (c) global fleet safety cap (existing circuit breaker path), and (d) a reduced refill rate (min_refill_qps) so we only prevent starvation; we don't increase tenant throughput beyond caps. Also if telemetry is stale for >2 windows we bias towards deny-with-retry rather than admit.
Peter Holtz (reviewer): Can we make the stale detection visible in dashboards? Otherwise oncall won't know why denials are changing.

Jordan Lee (author): Added burst_telemetry_stale_total + burst_telemetry_age_ms histogram and a smoothed gauge. Also added a short internal runbook note with "what to look at". I can add alert annotations in the follow-up PR (trying to keep this one hotfix-sized).
Allison Grant (reviewer): Please confirm label cardinality is bounded for the new metrics. No per-tenant labels on the histogram.
Jordan Lee (author): Confirmed: metrics are per pool (region, hardware_class, pool_id), no tenant_id labels. Tests include a guard to prevent adding tenant_id to stale metrics.
Mina Haddad (reviewer): Routing expects deterministic denial reasons. Any changes to reason codes?

Jordan Lee (author): No new reason codes. In stale mode we still return the same codes but we reduce consecutive denials caused solely by telemetry. Determinism preserved within a decision window given the same inputs; we include telemetry_age_ms only in internal debug logs/context.
Sean Gallagher (reviewer): What’s the rollback plan if this changes admission rates unexpectedly?

Jordan Lee (author): Feature flag burst.telemetry_smoothing_enabled; can be flipped off per region via config bundle. Also safe-guard: if smoothed signal diverges >20% from raw for >N samples, we log + fall back to raw (to avoid hiding real saturation).
Keiko Yamamoto (reviewer): Requested change: please clamp EWMA input to [0, pool_capacity] to avoid outliers; and add a test for negative capacity sample.
Jordan Lee (author): Done. Added clamp + unit test for negative sample and out-of-range sample. Also added comment explaining why we clamp (telemetry jitter + collector bugs).
Peter Holtz (reviewer): Approved after clamp + metrics. Please file a follow-up to add an alert when telemetry stale rate spikes.
Jordan Lee (author): Will do; tracking under ENG-4881 follow-up checklist.
Keiko Yamamoto (reviewer): Approved.
Mina Haddad (reviewer): Approved.
Sean Gallagher (reviewer): Approved; please ensure staged rollout per beta allowlist only.
Jordan Lee (author): Rollout is gated by existing per-tenant allowlist + env flag. Merging now.
release_notes: Burst admission controller now smooths fleet capacity telemetry and detects stale telemetry to prevent unfair burst denials during transient telemetry lag.
New internal metrics: burst_telemetry_stale_total, burst_free_capacity_smoothed, burst_telemetry_age_ms.

source.title: Add runbook links to experiment alerts + Console guardrail banners
source.author: Rafael Mendes
source.body: Problem: During beta we saw multiple slow triage loops where the on-call (and Support) had to ask around for the right runbook when an experiment guardrail alert fired (propagation lag, attribution drop rate, variant skew, etc.). We already have runbooks in Confluence, but the link isn’t present where the incident starts: alert payloads and the Console guardrail banners. This PR wires canonical runbook URLs into (1) alert payload annotations (so PagerDuty/Slack notifications carry a direct ‘Runbook’ link) and (2) Console guardrail banners on the experiment details/results pages. Scope: route experiments only (no global SRE alert conventions refactor in this PR). What changed: - Added a small, versioned mapping of {alert_key -> runbook_url} for experiment-related alerts (propagation lag, attribution drop rate, variant 0 traffic, allocation skew, p99 regression auto-stop). - Plumbed runbook_url through the alert/notification payload used by the guardrail evaluator and the notifications pipeline. - Updated Console guardrail banner model to accept an optional runbook_url and render a ‘View runbook’ CTA when present. - Added safeguards: if an alert key is unknown or the URL is missing/invalid, we omit the link (no broken UI, no malformed payload). - Added unit tests for mapping + URL validation and an integration test that asserts the runbook link is present end-to-end for a representative guardrail event. Runbook sources (canonical): - Experiment assignment debugging runbook: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPLAT/Experiment+Assignment+Debugging+Runbook - Experiment health dashboards spec (links out to dashboards/runbooks): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPLAT/Experiment+Health+Dashboards+Spec Notes: - This intentionally does not change Grafana rule definitions in observability-pack; it only ensures Redwood-originated notifications + Console surfaces carry the runbook URL. (Observability-pack PRs can independently add runbook_url annotations at rule level.) - URL allowlist: limited to confluence.redwoodinference.com to avoid arbitrary links in alert payloads. - Console UI is behind the existing route experiments feature flag; the banner rendering is a no-op if the backend doesn’t send runbook_url. Testing: - Unit: GuardrailAlertRunbookMapTest, UrlValidationTest - Integration: ExperimentGuardrailNotificationE2E (asserts runbook_url in notification payload + Console API response) Rollout: safe to ship with no config migrations; if we need to change runbook URLs, we can update the mapping and redeploy. Follow-ups: - Add runbook_url annotation in observability-pack alert rules so Grafana-native alerts match Redwood notifications. - Expand the mapping to cover non-experiment Rollouts alerts after GA if this proves useful.
commits: Add experiment alert key -> runbook URL mapping + URL validation
Plumb runbook_url through guardrail evaluator notification payload
Expose runbook_url in Console experiments guardrail API response
Console: render guardrail banner CTA when runbook_url is present
Tests: mapping/validation unit tests + E2E notification assertion
conversation: Sean Gallagher (review): Can we ensure we don’t ever emit arbitrary URLs? We’ve had issues where payload fields ended up being interpreted as clickable links in downstream tooling.
Rafael Mendes (author): Yep. Added a strict allowlist (confluence.redwoodinference.com only) + basic URL parsing. If validation fails, we omit runbook_url entirely.
Allison Grant (review): Naming: please keep the annotation key consistent with our existing conventions. We’ve been using runbook_url in alert payloads elsewhere.
Rafael Mendes (author): Updated to runbook_url everywhere (backend + Console response). Tests updated accordingly.
Logan Wright (review): Any risk of cardinality / metrics tagging impact?
Rafael Mendes (author): No metrics changes. This is only in notification payload + Console banner metadata.
Chloe Martin (review): Console API change looks good. One request: make sure the banner still renders cleanly if runbook_url missing. We have older guardrail rows in some environments.
Rafael Mendes (author): Added UI guard + API field is optional. Snapshot updated to cover missing runbook_url case.
Sean Gallagher (review): Please add a short note in the PR about observability-pack rule-level runbook annotations not being part of this change (to avoid confusion for on-call).
Rafael Mendes (author): Added in Notes section under scope.
source.release_notes: - Route experiments: guardrail alerts and Console guardrail banners now include direct links to the relevant on-call runbooks (when available).

source.title: Integrate Smart Routing with burst admission decisions (fallback + backpressure)
source.author: Elena Popov
source.description: Motivation: Dedicated burst capacity requires routing to consult the Burst Admission Controller before borrowing from shared burst pools, and to behave safely when burst is denied (protecting non-burst tenants + preserving determinism). This PR wires the routing/gateway path into the admission controller, adds deterministic policy evaluation inputs, and defines the fallback/backpressure behaviors for denied/timeout cases. Summary of behavior: For Dedicated tenants with burst enabled, routing computes a BurstContext (tenant_id, region, hw_class, baseline allocation id, requested_concurrency/requested_tokens estimate, priority tier) and calls the Burst Admission Controller synchronously at request admission time. If admitted, routing tags the request with the admission grant_id and burst_pool_id and routes to the burst pool endpoint group; if denied, routing falls back to baseline dedicated capacity when available (no pool borrowing) and otherwise applies backpressure (HTTP 429 with structured denial reason + Retry-After) rather than retrying across neighbors. Determinism: The admission request includes a stable request_key (derived from gateway request_id + model_route + tenant_id) and a policy_snapshot_hash from the control-plane config cache. Routing does not “shop around” across pools/regions/hardware classes. For the initial release, burst is strictly within-region and within hardware class. Failure modes: If the admission controller call times out or returns 5xx, routing treats it as a deny with reason=admission_unavailable and routes only to baseline dedicated capacity; if baseline is saturated, we backpressure with 503 (no burst) to avoid accidental neighbor impact. Observability: Adds routing-side counters and latency histograms for admission calls, denials by reason, and fallback usage, and propagates denial reason codes to the client error surface (aligned with pr-23795). Notes: This PR intentionally does not implement queueing-on-deny; the chosen semantics for v1 are immediate decision + client-driven retry using Retry-After guidance. Queueing can be revisited once we have stronger end-to-end SLO evidence and per-tenant buffering policies.
commits: routing: add burst admission client + request/response types
routing: integrate admission call into dedicated routing decision path
routing: implement fallback-to-baseline and backpressure semantics on deny
routing: propagate denial reason + retry_after to gateway error surface
observability: add metrics for admission latency, denies, fallbacks
tests: add routing unit tests + integration harness fakes for admission service
docs: add developer note on deterministic request_key and timeouts
source.implementation_notes: Decision flow: 1) If tenant.burst.enabled=false => unchanged routing behavior. 2) If enabled and request fits baseline capacity => route baseline (no admission call). 3) If enabled and baseline indicates saturation risk => call admission controller with BurstContext. 4a) ADMIT => route to burst pool, attach grant_id, emit admitted metric. 4b) DENY (pool_saturated | tenant_cap_reached | cooldown_active | fleet_safety_cap | policy_disabled | admission_unavailable) => attempt baseline route if capacity signal says safe; otherwise return backpressure. Backpressure mapping: When deny reason is capacity-related we return HTTP 429 and include x-redwood-burst-denial-reason plus a Retry-After (seconds) when provided by the admission controller; for non-retriable denials (policy_disabled) we return 403. Timeout budget: admission controller call is capped at 35ms p99 target; routing uses 50ms hard timeout and does not retry. This is to prevent tail amplification. Safety: If routing detects inconsistent config (missing hw_class / region mapping) it disables burst path for the request and logs an error with tenant_id + config version, then routes baseline only.
review_conversation: Mina Haddad: Can we guarantee we won’t bounce between baseline and burst across retries and create nondeterministic user experience? Please clarify request_key / idempotency strategy.

Elena Popov (author): Added request_key based on gateway request_id + route + tenant_id, and ensured routing never tries multiple pools. Also added note that client retries should reuse Idempotency-Key; gateway already does this for streaming reconnects. Updated docs/internal/burst/routing-integration-notes.md.
Ravi Menon: I’m concerned about calling admission for every request. Can we skip when baseline has headroom? Also please ensure this doesn’t regress hosted routing path.

Elena Popov (author): Added fast-path: only call admission when baseline capacity signal indicates saturation risk (or when request explicitly opts into burst via header set by internal batch jobs). Hosted path is untouched; dedicated-only code paths are gated by tenant plan + deployment mode checks. Added unit tests to assert hosted route selector does not import burst client.
Ethan Park: Please align deny reason handling with the reason codes from pr-23795 and avoid introducing new strings here. Also what’s the behavior when policy snapshot is stale?

Elena Popov (author): Updated mapping to use the shared enum + string constants from pkg/burst/denial_reasons.go. For stale snapshot, routing includes policy_snapshot_hash; admission controller returns deny reason=config_mismatch with retry_after=0 and we fall back to baseline only. Added metric burst_config_mismatch_total.

Peter Holtz: 503 vs 429 semantics matter for paging and client retry storms. Suggest 429 for capacity denial; 503 only for internal unavailability. Also add a circuit-breaker on admission timeouts to avoid cascading.

Elena Popov (author): Updated as suggested: 429 for capacity/cooldown/caps; 503 only when admission is unavailable AND baseline also cannot serve. Added a simple local circuit breaker: if admission timeouts > 5% over 1m window, skip admission calls for 2m and route baseline-only (emit burst_admission_cb_open).
source.testing: Unit: dedicated_route_selector tests cover (a) baseline headroom => no admission call, (b) admit => burst route selected + grant_id propagated, (c) deny tenant_cap_reached => baseline fallback when safe, (d) deny pool_saturated + baseline saturated => 429 w/ reason and Retry-After, (e) admission timeout => baseline-only; if baseline saturated => 503. Integration: added fake admission controller to routing integration harness to validate end-to-end header propagation and error mapping in gateway. Manual: replayed staging traffic sample with burst disabled and verified routing decisions identical (hash compare of route targets).
source.rollout_plan: Behind feature flags: routing.burst_admission.enabled (default off) and routing.burst_admission.region_allowlist. Enable in staging for 1 tenant, then dogfood in us-east/h100. SRE to monitor burst_admission_latency_ms p95 and denial-rate dashboards before expanding allowlist. Rollback: flip flag to disable admission calls; routing returns to baseline-only behavior.
source.release_notes: Dedicated: Burst-enabled tenants now consult burst admission before borrowing capacity. When burst is denied, routing falls back to baseline when possible or returns structured backpressure (429) with denial reasons and retry hints.

source.title: Route experiments: guardrail evaluator (soft-stop) + notifications
source.author: Rafael Mendes
source.description: This adds the initial guardrail evaluation pipeline for route-level experiments. The evaluator computes guardrail status per {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id} from the existing experiment rollups and updates experiment status to DEGRADED when thresholds are breached. In this PR the system is intentionally soft-stop only: we do NOT stop routing traffic or mutate allocation automatically. Instead we (1) mark the experiment as DEGRADED with a reason payload suitable for Console, (2) emit audit + internal events, and (3) notify the owning team via Slack (and optionally email via notifier integration). Motivation: beta customers and internal teams need a consistent way to catch regressions quickly (p99 latency, error rate, cost/token) without relying on bespoke dashboards. This unblocks Console guardrail banners and the first-pass on-call playbooks without taking on the operational risk of automatic hard-stop. Follow-ups: optional hard-stop + kill switch (tracked separately), alert tuning, and quality-eval guardrails once eval scores are wired. Implementation overview: - Adds GuardrailEvaluator job in experiment-service that runs every 60s per shard, evaluating active experiments only. - Fetches aggregated metrics from analytics rollup tables (5m window by default; configurable to 1m/15m). - Compares metrics to guardrails in experiment config (per-variant thresholds; defaults applied when unspecified). - Updates experiment_status table with: status=HEALTHY|DEGRADED, last_evaluated_at, breached_guardrails list, and a stable incident_key for de-duping notifications. - Emits OTel span and a metric: route_experiments.guardrail_breaches_total{guardrail_type, route_id, experiment_id}. - Sends notifications via internal notifier service (Slack channel + message templates) with rate limiting + de-dupe (no more than once per incident_key per 30m). Guardrails included in this PR: latency_p99_ms, error_rate, cost_per_1k_tokens_usd, throughput_drop_pct (relative to control when available; otherwise absolute threshold). Non-goals: - No automatic stop / rollback (soft-stop only). - No statistical significance or long-horizon analysis. - No customer-defined custom metrics. title: Route experiments: guardrail evaluator (soft-stop) + notifications
preview: This adds the initial guardrail evaluation pipeline for route-level experiments. The evaluator computes guardrail status per {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id} from the existing experiment rollups and updates experiment status to DEGRADED when thresholds are breached. In this PR the system is intentionally soft-stop only: we do NOT stop routing traffic or mutate allocation automatically. Instead we (1) mark the experiment as DEGRADED with a reason payload suitable for Console, (2) emit audit + internal events, and (3) notify the owning team via Slack (and optionally email via notifier integration). Motivation: beta customers and internal teams need a consistent way to catch regressions quickly (p99 latency, error rate, cost/token) without relying on bespoke dashboards. This unblocks Console guardrail banners and the first-pass on-call playbooks without taking on the operational risk of automatic hard-stop. Follow-ups: optional hard-stop + kill switch (tracked separately), alert tuning, and quality-eval guardrails once eval scores are wired. Implementation overview: - Adds GuardrailEvaluator job in experiment-service that runs every 60s per shard, evaluating active experiments only. - Fetches aggregated metrics from analytics rollup tables (5m window by default; configurable to 1m/15m). - Compares metrics to guardrails in experiment config (per-variant thresholds; defaults applied when unspecified). - Updates experiment_status table with: status=HEALTHY|DEGRADED, last_evaluated_at, breached_guardrails list, and a stable incident_key for de-duping notifications. - Emits OTel span and a metric: route_experiments.guardrail_breaches_total{guardrail_type, route_id, experiment_id}. - Sends notifications via internal notifier service (Slack channel + message templates) with rate limiting + de-dupe (no more than once per incident_key per 30m). Guardrails included in this PR: latency_p99_ms, error_rate, cost_per_1k_tokens_usd, throughput_drop_pct (relative to control when available; otherwise absolute threshold). Non-goals: - No automatic stop / rollback (soft-stop only). - No statistical significance or long-horizon analysis. - No customer-defined custom metrics. Safety/operability: - Feature-flagged: route_experiments.guardrails_evaluator_enabled (default OFF). - Writes are idempotent and bounded; evaluator skips when analytics rollups are stale beyond 10m. - Notification rate limiting is per {org_id, experiment_id, guardrail_type}. - Adds runbook links in Slack payload (will be wired into alerts in follow-up). Testing: unit tests for threshold evaluation, de-dupe, staleness handling, and control-vs-variant deltas; integration test against local analytics fixture tables; dogfooded in staging with synthetic latency injection. source.title: Burst: standardize denial reason codes + surface to routing/gateway
source.author: Elena Popov
source.description: Motivation: During burst beta we’ve had multiple cases where customers/Support see generic 429/overload errors without actionable context (e.g., cap reached vs fleet safety vs cooldown). This PR standardizes a small, stable set of burst denial reason codes in the admission controller response and propagates them through routing -> gateway so we can (a) render clear customer-facing error messages, (b) expose denial breakdowns in Console and dashboards, and (c) reduce Support back-and-forth by making the denial cause inspectable. Summary of changes: 1) Introduce a versioned enum `BurstDenialReason` in `common/capacity` shared types with explicit semantics and non-overlapping categories. 2) Admission controller returns `decision=DENY` with `reason_code` and optional `retry_after_ms` for retryable denials (e.g., COOLDOWN_ACTIVE / POOL_SATURATED). 3) Routing stores the reason code in request context and includes it in structured logs + trace attributes; for HTTP responses we map denial to a consistent error payload and set `x-redwood-burst-denial-reason` header for Dedicated tenants (guarded behind feature flag). 4) Gateway maps denial reason codes to a stable `error.code` string (e.g., `burst_denied_pool_saturated`) and preserves existing status codes (429 for capacity-style denials, 503 for internal admission errors/timeouts) to avoid breaking clients. 5) Add Prometheus counter `redwood_burst_denials_total{reason=...}` with strict label set; no tenant_id label to avoid cardinality blowups. 6) Tests: unit tests for mapping table, golden tests for error payloads, and an integration test that asserts determinism: same inputs -> same denial code. Backwards compatibility: Existing clients that only look at status code/message are unaffected. Header + structured field are additive and behind `burst.denial_reasons.enabled` flag default OFF until rollout. Reason code set (initial): `TENANT_BURST_DISABLED`, `TENANT_CAP_REACHED`, `MAX_BURST_RATIO_EXCEEDED`, `COOLDOWN_ACTIVE`, `POOL_SATURATED`, `FLEET_SAFETY_CAP`, `TELEMETRY_STALE`, `POLICY_EVAL_ERROR`, `ADMISSION_TIMEOUT`. Follow-ups: Console surface (ENG-4855) will use the new read API + denial breakdown; Support playbook update in ENG-4881 to align messaging and recommended retry/backoff per reason.
commits: common(capacity): add BurstDenialReason enum + docs for semantics
admission-controller: return reason_code + retry_after_ms on deny paths
routing: propagate denial reason into ctx, structured logs, trace attrs
gateway: map denial reasons to stable error.code + optional response header
metrics: add redwood_burst_denials_total{reason} and mapping tests
tests: golden payloads + integration determinism coverage
source.implementation_notes: Reason code mapping intentionally avoids leaking fleet-internal details beyond what customers need to act. `POOL_SATURATED` is used when the regional/hw-class burst pool has no borrowable capacity at the moment; `FLEET_SAFETY_CAP` is used when global circuit breaker / safety cap is tripped (customer action: reduce burst or retry later). `TELEMETRY_STALE` is treated as retryable with exponential backoff; we include `retry_after_ms=250` default (configurable) to reduce thundering herds. `POLICY_EVAL_ERROR` and `ADMISSION_TIMEOUT` are surfaced as 503 with `error.code=burst_admission_unavailable` and do not include a denial header to avoid misclassification. Routing fallback behavior remains unchanged: if a request is eligible for baseline dedicated capacity it will continue on baseline path; only the burst portion is denied, and the response indicates denial only when the request cannot be served within the tenant’s baseline envelope.
source.api_contract: When a burst request is denied, gateway responds with HTTP 429 and JSON: `{ "error": { "type": "capacity_error", "code": "burst_denied_pool_saturated", "message": "Burst capacity is temporarily unavailable (pool saturated).", "retry_after_ms": 250 } }`. Additionally, when `burst.denial_reasons.enabled=true`, gateway sets `x-redwood-burst-denial-reason: POOL_SATURATED`. Reason codes are stable identifiers intended for dashboards, support tooling, and customer troubleshooting; do not localize them.
source.rollout_plan: 1) Merge behind flag (this PR). 2) Enable in staging for Dedicated burst beta tenants. 3) Validate metrics + no header leakage for non-Dedicated traffic. 4) Enable for beta cohort by region (us-east H100 first), monitor `redwood_burst_denials_total` and 429 rate. 5) After Support playbook update, enable header + error.code mapping globally for burst-enabled Dedicated tenants.
source.ci_summary: Buildkite: monorepo build (pass), unit tests (pass), lint (pass), integration tests (pass, 1 retry due to flaky timing), security scan (pass).
source.review_conversation: Mina Haddad (routing): Can we guarantee determinism for the reason codes under telemetry lag? I’m worried about flapping between POOL_SATURATED and TELEMETRY_STALE. Elena Popov (author): Good call. Updated admission controller to prefer TELEMETRY_STALE whenever freshness window exceeded, before saturation checks. Added an integration test asserting stable reason selection with synthetic stale timestamps. Ravi Menon: Please keep status codes unchanged; clients may treat 503 differently than 429. Also, header should be behind a flag until we confirm no proxy strips/rewrites. Elena: Kept 429/503 mapping as described; added `burst.denial_reasons.enabled` gate and default OFF. Ethan Park: Enum needs to live in shared types, not service-local, so policy engine and control plane can reference it later. Elena: Moved to `common/capacity` and updated imports. Allison Grant: Metrics label `reason` is okay, but please ensure there is no tenant_id label and cap the set. Elena: Confirmed no tenant labels; reason codes are enum-backed, and metric registration rejects unknown strings. Peter Holtz: Can we add a note about retry guidance per reason (cooldown vs pool saturated)? Elena: Added `retry_after_ms` for retryable denials and documented guidance in `burst_denial_reason.md`. Approvals: Mina Haddad approved after determinism test; Ravi Menon approved after status code confirmation; Ethan Park approved with shared-types change; Allison Grant approved with label constraints; Peter Holtz approved with retry guidance.
source.release_notes: Internal: Admission controller now provides standardized denial reason codes and optional retry_after hints; routing/gateway can surface these for Dedicated burst debugging behind a feature flag.

source.title: Fix missing experiment attribution on streaming retries
source.author: Caleb Johnson
source.release_notes: Fixes a bug where experiment_id/variant_id attribution could be dropped on server-side streaming retry paths, improving correctness of per-variant metrics and traces in Console.
source.description: Context: During the route experiments beta we saw a subset of streaming requests (SSE) lose experiment attribution after a retry, which caused (a) undercounted per-variant traffic, (b) misleading guardrail charts, and (c) confusing traces where the initial attempt was tagged but the retry attempt was not. This was escalated via SUP-24877 and corroborated by staging attribution-drop dashboards. Root cause: Our streaming retry path reconstructs a new RequestContext / SpanContext for the retry attempt. The base routing decision (including experiment assignment) was persisted on the first attempt, but the retry code path re-evaluated portions of the request pipeline and did not copy experiment dimensions into the retry context. In some cases we also emitted the streaming response headers from the retry attempt before experiment tags were re-attached, so x-redwood-experiment-id / x-redwood-experiment-variant were missing even though the route was in an active experiment. Changes: (1) Persist experiment assignment (experiment_id, variant_id, config_version) into the per-request RetryState and rehydrate it into the retry RequestContext before any OTel spans/metrics are emitted. (2) Ensure the streaming response header writer reads attribution from RequestContext (not transient span tags) so retries remain consistent. (3) Add a guard to prevent re-bucketing on retry unless the original assignment is absent (maintains determinism and prevents variant flipping mid-stream). (4) Add coverage: integration test exercising streaming + injected mid-stream network failure + retry, asserting that experiment dimensions remain stable across attempts and are present on emitted spans/metrics and response debug headers. Safety/rollout: This is behind the existing route experiments feature flag; behavior only changes when retries occur. Added a metric counter for attribution_rehydrated_on_retry to validate impact in canary. Linked work: ADR-048 (attribution schema), PR-28470 (request context plumbing).
commits: router/streaming: persist experiment assignment in RetryState
otel: rehydrate experiment dimensions before span creation on retry
gateway: write experiment headers from RequestContext for streaming responses
tests: add integration test for streaming retry attribution stability
docs/dev: add troubleshooting note for attribution drop on retries (internal)
changed_areas: OTel span/metric tagging for streaming attempts
API gateway header writer for streaming responses
review_conversation: Logan Wright (Telemetry): The approach makes sense. Can we confirm we’re not increasing cardinality by adding per-attempt tags? Also please ensure we don’t re-bucket on retry; variant flipping would be a nightmare for attribution.

Caleb Johnson (author): Good call. This keeps the same attribution tags per attempt; no new dimensions. Also added a hard guard: if RetryState has assignment, we skip bucketing entirely and just rehydrate. Added assertions in the integration test to prevent variant changes across attempts.
Jordan Lee (Policy Engine): Why not always re-evaluate bucketing for correctness if config changed mid-request?

Caleb Johnson (author): For streaming, we prioritize per-request determinism. Re-bucketing mid-stream can cause headers/metrics mismatch and violate stickiness expectations. If config changes after the first attempt, the next request will pick it up; this keeps a single request consistent.
Vikram Singh (Data plane): Can you add a log/debug field showing config_version used for the rehydrated path? Helps oncall debugging.
Caleb Johnson (author): Added experiment_config_version to the internal debug log line on retry and exposed it in the internal assignment explain endpoint payload (when present).
Sean Gallagher (SRE): Please add a metric so we can validate the fix during beta without digging through logs. Also ensure it doesn’t spam on high retry rates.

Caleb Johnson (author): Added attribution_rehydrated_on_retry counter and it’s only emitted once per retry attempt. No labels beyond route_id (already present elsewhere) and experiment_id/variant_id (existing dims).

source.title: Dedicated rollouts: implement promote/abort actions + audit events (idempotent, concurrency-safe)
source.author: Benji Okafor
release_notes: Control plane now supports explicit promote/abort actions for DedicatedPoolRolloutPlan with auditable, idempotent semantics.
Audit events emitted for rollout actions (create/update already existed; this adds promote/abort + override scaffolding).
source.description:
Context: Dedicated staged rollouts need deterministic, auditable stage-by-stage promotion and a safe abort path. This PR implements the action endpoints on the dedicated-pool-rollouts service, wires audit event emission, and adds correctness guardrails (idempotency keys + optimistic concurrency) to make promotion safe under retries and operator concurrency.

What’s included: (1) New actions: POST /v1/dedicated_pools/{pool_id}/rollout_plans/{plan_id}:promote and POST /v1/dedicated_pools/{pool_id}/rollout_plans/{plan_id}:abort. (2) Deterministic state transitions + validation: only RUNNING|HELD plans can be promoted; only RUNNING|HELD can be aborted; COMPLETED/ABORTED are terminal. Promotion increments stage_index (monotonic), records promoted_at, and snapshots gate evaluation summary at time of action. Abort transitions to ABORTED and reverts active stage routing intent to the last known-good baseline (control plane intent only; data plane applies via existing reconciler). source.title: [LTS 1.18] Fix Sev1 crash in KV cache compaction (backport)
source.author: Samir Qureshi
source.body:
Context
This is a targeted backport to runtime-1.18-lts for a Sev1 crash in KV cache compaction reported during LTS pilot validation and via SUP-18544. The crash manifests as a use-after-free / invalid access during compaction when a block is concurrently scheduled for eviction under memory pressure. We are treating this as LTS-eligible (correctness + crash) and release-blocking for 1.18.1-lts RC.

Root cause
Compaction iterates over a segment list while eviction can detach/free segments after a memory-pressure callback. On main, this was mitigated by refcounted pinning and a stricter lock ordering (compaction acquires segment_lock before eviction_lock). In the 1.18 LTS branch, we still had the older code path where compaction relied on "best-effort" pointer stability and a non-atomic "in_compaction" flag, which is insufficient under bursty workloads with aggressive eviction.

Fix
- Introduce segment pinning during compaction: compaction now takes a strong ref to the segment header before walking blocks; eviction checks pin count and defers freeing until pins drop.
- Enforce lock ordering to avoid a rare deadlock we saw in a local stress test when trying the naive fix (documented in code comment).
- Harden guardrails: if compaction detects a segment state transition mid-walk (evict_pending), it bails out and retries rather than continuing with stale offsets.

Why safe for LTS
- Minimal diff relative to mainline fix; avoids bringing in unrelated KV cache changes that landed after 1.18 branch cut.
- No behavior change on the steady state path except an extra atomic inc/dec around segments being compacted.
- Added focused tests + ran perf gates to validate no measurable regression within LTS thresholds.

Testing
- Added unit test that reproduces the crash by interleaving compaction + eviction on a mocked clock and deterministic executor.
- Added GPU smoke test variant that forces compaction every N tokens with small KV budget to trigger frequent eviction.
- Ran scheduler regression suite (release branch) to ensure no cross-component fallout.

Perf validation notes (perf-canary)
Benchmarks ran on A100-80G and H100, workloads: chat-mixed (short+long), summarization-long, and embeddings-batch. Results vs runtime-1.18.0-lts baseline: 
- Throughput: within -0.3% to +0.2% (noise)
- p95 latency: +0.4% worst-case on summarization-long (within gate)
- Memory: no increase in steady-state KV footprint; transient pinning increases peak pinned segments by <= 2 in stress mode only

Rollout / release notes
- This should be called out under "Stability" in 1.18.1-lts release notes: "Fixes a rare but high-severity crash in KV cache compaction under eviction pressure."
- No config changes required.

References
- Jira: SUP-18544
- Related LTS epic: ENG-4821

Checklist
- [x] Backport eligibility confirmed (Sev1 crash)
- [x] Focused regression test added
- [x] GPU smoke test executed on release branch
- [x] Perf-canary run and within LTS thresholds
- [x] SRE notified for pilot rollout monitoring
- [x] Changelog note drafted for 1.18.1-lts
commits: backport: pin kv segments during compaction to prevent concurrent free
backport: enforce lock ordering between eviction and compaction paths
tests: add deterministic eviction/compaction interleaving reproducer
tests: add gpu-smoke scenario forcing frequent compaction under low kv budget
docs: add inline comments on LTS risk rationale + retry behavior
conversation: 2025-02-18 Connor O'Brien: Can you confirm this is the same fix as mainline and not pulling in the newer allocator changes? Also please add a short note on why we consider this LTS-safe.

2025-02-18 Samir Qureshi: Yes—this is the minimal subset of the mainline fix (pinning + lock ordering). I avoided the allocator refactor and the newer segment layout changes. Added an explicit "Why safe for LTS" section in the PR body + code comment in compaction.cc.

2025-02-19 Hiro Tanaka: The lock ordering comment mentions a deadlock risk—can you add a regression test or at least an assertion to ensure we don't take eviction_lock then segment_lock anywhere else?

2025-02-19 Samir Qureshi: Added a debug-only assertion in eviction.cc to enforce ordering for the callsites we control in LTS. Full static enforcement is larger than I'd like for LTS, but this will catch regressions quickly in CI.

2025-02-20 Leila Farouk: The deterministic test looks good. Please ensure it fails on the pre-fix revision (so we know it's actually covering the crash) and add it to the release branch test suite filter.
2025-02-20 Samir Qureshi: Confirmed it fails on pre-fix (segfault in 30-60 iterations). Added it to the kv_cache test target and tagged it as "lts_release" so it runs in release-branch CI.
2025-02-21 Sean Gallagher: From SRE side: please paste the perf-canary summary artifact link and call out any p95 deltas. Also, should we consider this patch mandatory for pilot accounts?

2025-02-21 Samir Qureshi: Perf summary is in the CI check output (perf-canary gate) and copied into the PR under "Perf validation notes". Given this is a Sev1 crash, I'd recommend we mark 1.18.1-lts as mandatory for accounts on 1.18.0-lts that have compaction enabled (default) and are running near KV limits.
2025-02-22 Noah Patel: Looks correct and aligned with main. One nit: the retry loop should have a bounded attempt count to avoid pathological churn under continuous eviction.
2025-02-22 Samir Qureshi: Good catch—added max_retry=3 with a metric increment when exceeded; compaction will defer to next scheduled cycle after that. Updated tests accordingly.
2025-02-22 Noah Patel: Approved.
2025-02-22 Hiro Tanaka: Approved.
2025-02-22 Connor O'Brien: Approved; please coordinate with the 1.18.1-lts release PR for release note inclusion.

source.title: Expedited rejoin stabilizer and controlled load-trim hotfix
source.author: Sofia Ramirez
source.description: Motivation: Following a recent outage reconstruction (SUP-4512) we observed an oscillation during regional rejoin where multiple nodes aggressively tried to reclaim tenant KV slots, triggering cascade redistributions and transient SLO violations. This PR introduces a short-term hotfix to: 1) introduce a gentle rejoin backoff window (per-tenant jittered delay + exponential cap) to avoid synchronized reclaims; 2) add a controlled load-trim path that softly sheds low-priority traffic during rejoin windows; and 3) emit new telemetry to make oscillation and rejoin latency visible in the Console. This change is intentionally conservative: defaults apply only during detected rejoin epochs and are disabled outside of a rejoin recovery window. Changes: - runtime/scheduler/rejoin_coordinator.go: add rejoin state machine with randomized tenant backoff and exponential cooldown logic; add tests for staggered rejoin timings. - runtime/load_trim/trimmer.go: implement priority-aware soft-trimmer that signals request proxies to shed non-critical classes via header flag and emits approximate token savings. - controlplane/telemetry/v2: add rejoin_epoch metric, tenant_rejoin_backoff_seconds histogram, and soft_trim_shed_count counter. - config/defaults.yaml: new toggle rejoin_relay.enabled (default true for this rollout window) and rejoin_relay.default_backoff_ms. - docs/ops/rejoin-playbook.md: updated playbook with diagnostic queries and rollback steps. Rollout plan: - Stage: feature gated behind rejoin_relay.enabled. - Canary: enable for 5% of tenants in us-west-2 for 2 hours, observe rejoin_epoch and token/shed metrics. - Ramp: 25% after 6 hours if no increase in 95th latency or error rate; full rollout after 24h stability. Backout: flip rejoin_relay.enabled=false; will disable new path immediately. Checklist: - [x] unit tests for coordinator state transitions - [x] e2e simulation harness run for tenant rejoin storm - [x] metrics wired to Console dashboards - [x] docs updated - [x] post-merge telemetry alert to watch for increased shed volume Monitoring and alerts: new SLO probe rule added to detect >3x baseline soft_trim_shed_count in 10m window; alert routed to SRE runbook. Notes: This is a hotfix to reduce immediate customer impact while we finalize a larger, model-aware eviction redesign. See ENG-9821 for the long-running redesign epic.
commits: a1f3b6b: introduce RejoinCoordinator and unit tests
b2d9f12: implement PrioritySoftTrimmer and proxy header contract
c3e4aa3: add rejoin telemetry and dashboard wiring
d4b7f21: update defaults, add ops playbook, and small test fixes
review_comments: Liam Chen: Suggest capping the exponential backoff at 30s to avoid long tenant cold-starts; also add a histogram bucket for 10s-30s in tenant_rejoin_backoff_seconds.
Sofia Ramirez: Added cap to 30s and new histogram buckets in telemetry commit b3. Verified in e2e simulation.
Priya Kapoor: Code looks good. Please ensure load-trim header uses the established proxy contract (X-Redwood-Trim: soft|hard).
Sofia Ramirez: Updated trimmer to emit X-Redwood-Trim: soft and added proxy-side acceptance test.
Ethan Park: Ran canary in staging for 2h, rejoin_epoch spikes reduced and 95th latency unchanged. Approving for merge.
source.release_notes: Hotfix: Add tenant rejoin coordination with jittered backoff and a priority-aware soft load-trim to reduce rejoin oscillations. Includes telemetry and ops playbook. Default enabled during hotfix rollout.

source.title: Backfill tooling for 2025Q1 unit economics aggregates (idempotent + throttled)
source.author: Mei Lin
source.description: Context: Finance needs QTD and quarter-close reporting in the internal Unit Economics dashboard, but the hourly aggregation job only covered data from the initial cutover onward. This PR adds an idempotent backfill tool to populate historical aggregates for 2025Q1 (and generally any arbitrary time window) while protecting the warehouse and avoiding double-counting. Scope: (1) Adds a backfill CLI (unit-econ-backfill) that replays the same aggregation logic as the hourly job over a time range; (2) Implements idempotency via per-hour partition overwrite semantics and a backfill_runs audit table; (3) Adds throttling (max concurrent partitions, adaptive sleep on warehouse pressure) and a maintenance-window mode; (4) Adds verification queries and a reconciliation helper view to compare pre/post backfill totals; (5) Updates runbook docs referenced by alerts. Idempotency model: the backfill operates on canonical hourly partitions (dt_hour UTC) in the materialized aggregate tables. For each dt_hour, it writes to a staging table and then atomically swaps/overwrites the target partition. A backfill_runs table records (run_id, requested_range, effective_partitions, rate_card_version, git_sha, started_at, completed_at, status) for auditability and for detecting partial runs. Concurrency + safety: default max_concurrency=4, with a --warehouse-safe flag that drops to 2 and increases sleep on query timeouts; --dry-run prints partition plan and estimated work; --resume uses backfill_runs to skip completed partitions; --force allows reprocessing (for rate card updates or logic changes) but still overwrites per-hour partitions to avoid duplication. Notes: We explicitly do not backfill raw metering events; we backfill derived aggregates only. This is intended for internal reporting and reconciliation and is gated behind internal authz. Testing: Added unit tests for partition planning/idempotency and integration tests against a local warehouse test container with seeded metering + rate card fixtures. Rollout: Run once for 2025-01-01..2025-03-31 (inclusive) in the analytics maintenance window; SRE will monitor warehouse saturation dashboards; Finance will validate QTD totals using the reconciliation view (linked below). Follow-ups: If Finance requests prior quarters, we can reuse the same tool; we may want a weekly scheduled backfill for late-arriving metering data, but that is out of scope for v1.
commits: Add unit-econ-backfill CLI scaffold + config parsing
Implement hourly partition planner and staging write path
Add idempotent overwrite for dt_hour partitions + audit table
Add throttling/backoff and maintenance window mode
Integration tests with seeded fixtures + verification helpers
Docs: runbook updates and example backfill commands
conversation: Logan Wright (review): Can we guarantee no double-count if a run is interrupted mid-hour? Also please confirm that the planner always uses canonical UTC hours; Finance had day-boundary confusion previously.

Mei Lin (author): Good call. Writes are per dt_hour partition; each partition is computed independently and committed via staging->swap. If interrupted, already-swapped partitions are complete and audit-marked; remaining are untouched. Planner is strictly UTC (trunc to hour) and we store dt_hour in UTC; added a test that input ranges with non-UTC offsets normalize correctly.

Caleb Johnson (review): We need guardrails for warehouse load. INT-1059 came from a prior backfill causing timeouts. Can we default to a safer concurrency and add adaptive backoff on warehouse errors?

Mei Lin (author): Added --warehouse-safe (defaults to on in prod config) that limits concurrency to 2 and increases exponential backoff on timeout/too-many-queries. Also emits per-partition latency metrics and logs a warning when p95 query time exceeds threshold, suggesting to pause/resume.

Cole Summers (review): Please include the rate_card_version used in the run metadata so Finance can tie results to the 2025Q1 rate card. Also ensure the tool fails closed if the rate card lookup returns multiple active versions for the same date range.

Mei Lin (author): Done. backfill_runs now records rate_card_version and git_sha; runner validates a single resolved rate card version for the requested window unless --allow-multi-rate-card is explicitly set (intended for mid-quarter updates).
Sean Gallagher (review): Can we run this without holding long locks? Partition overwrite via swap worries me if readers are hitting the materialized views.

Mei Lin (author): Swap is implemented as an atomic partition replace supported by the warehouse (metadata operation); the staging write is the heavy step. Also added a note in the runbook to run during the analytics maintenance window and to invalidate cached dashboard queries afterwards.
Aisha Bello (comment): Please add metrics for backfill progress and failure rate so we can alert if it stalls.

Mei Lin (author): Added unit_econ.backfill.partitions_total/complete/failed gauges and a heartbeat log every N partitions. Alerts not included here; will wire up in the observability-pack PR separately.
Logan Wright (approval): Looks good after UTC normalization + idempotency tests. Approved.
Sean Gallagher (approval): Approved with the maintenance-window guidance and safer defaults.
source.merge_outcome: Merged to main; backfill scheduled for next maintenance window (Fri 02/23 02:00–04:00 UTC) with SRE monitoring and Finance verification.
release_notes: Internal: Added idempotent backfill tooling for unit economics aggregates (supports 2025Q1 historical coverage).
Internal: Added reconciliation helper views and documented safe run procedure + resume semantics.

source.title: responses-adapter: propagate determinism/seed headers and structured-output annotations
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: Motivation: The OpenAI Responses variants that customers adopt increasingly rely on explicit determinism/seed directives and lightweight structured-output annotations passed through the HTTP layer. Without preserving those hints across our OpenAI-compat adapter we observed divergences between direct upstream behavior and Redwood-hosted responses in customer compatibility testing (see ENG-4821). This PR implements two coordinated changes in the adapter and runtime surface to improve compatibility and reduce customer surprises: 1) deterministic/seed propagation: accept and translate OpenAI-style determinism flags (seed, deterministic, x-seed) present as either JSON fields in the Responses body or as HTTP headers, and persist them through the routing, reassembly, and streaming layers so that streaming reassembly uses the same determinism hint used by the model selection logic; 2) structured-output annotations: accept structured output schema hints (openai_schema, schema_hint, or x-output-schema) and surface them to the structured-output postprocessor and the tool-call router. Implementation summary: - HTTP adapter: new header handling for X-OpenAI-Seed, X-OpenAI-Deterministic and X-OpenAI-Output-Schema; these are normalized to internal headers X-Redwood-Seed and X-Redwood-Output-Schema during internal RPCs so that downstream components see a consistent key. - Payload parsing: Responses-compatible body parsing now reads top-level "determinism" and "seed" keys in addition to existing generation params and merges them with header values (headers take precedence). - Streaming reassembly: streaming reassembly now attaches a deterministic-context token to each stream chunk and includes seed metadata in the final reassembly envelope so consumers and logs can assert determinism and replay. This prevents a class of flaky stream reorder bugs seen during canary. - Tool/function calling: the toolcall envelope now carries the propagated schema hint on the function metadata so third-party tool runners can perform schema-aware validation/dispatch. - Backwards compatibility: adapter will ignore unknown header keys and continues to accept legacy openai-style body shapes; a compatibility shim maps older "seed_hint" fields to the new internal header where detected. - Tests and validations: unit tests for adapter parsing, integration tests exercising streaming reassembly with seeded responses, and an end-to-end canary against a mocked OpenAI-responses backend were added. Checklist: - [x] Add header normalization in adapter layer - [x] Merge seed into routing decisions (with tests) - [x] Attach seed/annotations to streaming reassembly envelope - [x] Surface schema hint to toolcall metadata - [x] Add integration tests and canary job - [x] Update docs/compat notes (short note in docs site) Related: ENG-4821 (compat issues during customer test runs), ENG-4835 (structured output rollout plan). Breaking change: none; feature is opt-in via header/body keys. Rollout plan: feature gated behind a config flag responses.adapter.enable_seed_passthrough (default off). We enabled the flag in the dedicated canary namespaces and verified downstream regression metrics before merging.

b1e5f2d: runtime: attach deterministic-context token to stream envelope
c7f14e8: tools: include output-schema in toolcall metadata
d4a21b5: tests: add integration harness for seeded streaming reassembly
e8e55c2: docs: compat notes for Responses seed/annotations
review_thread: Ethan Park (reviewer): Can you confirm headers take precedence over body-level seed when both are present? The compatibility matrix in ENG-4821 assumes header-first behavior.
Maya Chen (author): Yes — updated adapter/parse.go: headers override body keys. Added explicit unit tests to cover both orders.
Priya Nair (reviewer): Suggest adding a short-lived metric that counts how often seed is provided via header vs body so we can monitor usage post-rollout.
Maya Chen (author): Added seed_source metric and emitted tags (header|body|none); seen in canary results. PTAL.
Luca Romano (approver): Integration looks good. I ran the canary against the mocked backend and confirmed identical token stream orders with seed set. Approving for merge.

source.title: Enforce credential-usage-policy: KMS claim translator & compact audit zipper
source.author: Lena Ortega
source.description:
Motivation: tighten operator credential usage and KMS selection so that operator/agent tokens cannot be used to access keys outside approved jurisdictions, while keeping audit trails compact and verifiable. This PR adds a lightweight RBAC v2 credential-usage policy evaluator, a KMS claim translator that maps operator claims to region-bound key selectors, and a compact audit zipper that collapses repetitive audit entries while retaining a cryptographic verification header for later replay. These features together address customer requests for stricter jurisdictional controls (Linear ENG-2148) and an incident postmortem recommendation to reduce audit storage costs without losing forensic detail (SEC-1203).

What this changes: single continuous description below —
- RBAC: add new policy predicate credential_usage.allowed_actions and integrate into decision path for operator-scoped tokens. The evaluator runs as an early deny guard in request admission (priority before quotas/ratelimit). Default deny-if-absent semantics for cross-region operations.
- KMS claim translator: a new translation layer that consumes token operator_claims.region_hints and service tags to select KMS key aliases with region affinity. It emits a signed kms_selection_proof header that downstream keystore proxies verify; translation includes a fallback chain (primary region -> tenant region -> global soft-fail) configurable by policy.
- Region pinning: request routing honors the translator's region_hint; requests with explicit residency flags are pinned to matching capacity pools and a non-matching route results in a structured audit event + rate-limited denial to avoid silent fallback.
- Compact audit zipper: stream-side component that collapses repeated audit lines (same actor/action/resource within configurable window) into a single compressed envelope while emitting an audit_index entry. The envelope includes a Merkle-root-like digest and a timestamped nonce; raw events are stored for 72 hours then replaced by the envelope for long-term retention to satisfy e-discovery while saving S3/DB costs.
- Telemetry & observability: add metrics for denied_credential_usage, kms_translation_latency, audit_zipper_compression_ratio, and a per-tenant residency_pin_rate. Traces include the translator decision span and the audit zipper span for post-incident timelines.

Checklist (implemented):
- [x] RBAC predicate + admission hook
- [x] KMS claim translator and signed selection header
- [x] Routing pin enforcement and structured deny event
- [x] Audit zipper compression logic and retention lifecycle
- [x] Unit + integration tests for policy and translator
- [x] Migration guide and feature flag (off by default)

Compatibility/rollback: Feature gated under feature.flags.credential_usage_enforcer. Rolling back flips the gate and reverts request admission to previous path; translator has a "passthrough" mode to preserve behavior during canary. Migration notes are in docs/security/credential-usage.md.

Related: linear ENG-2148, SEC-1203, and internal runbook RBAC-R1. No customer-facing API changes; header kms_selection_proof is additive and will be ignored by older proxies.

d9c7bb4: implement kms claim translator + selection proof signer (kms/translator.go)
ff4e2c9: audit zipper stream implementation and indexer (audit/zipper.go, audit/indexer.go)
b7a9d11: add metrics and tracing spans for translator/auditzipper (observability/metrics.go)
c6d33a5: tests for admission hook, translator fallbacks, and zipper retention (tests/*)

admission/hooks.go — register credential usage early deny hook
routing/pinner.go — honor residency pin and emit structured deny
Makefile — add target for audit-test harness
review_comments: Ravi Patel: high-level LGTM; please call out performance impact on p99 path for per-request translator (we saw earlier regressions in similar code).
Lena Ortega: measured translator p99 = +0.9ms in canary with signing; added a micro-benchmark and introduced caching for identical claims (cache TTL 30s).

Maya Chen: audit zipper looks good; verify that the compressed envelope contains non-repudiation header for legal discovery. Also request a link to the retention policy and a tests for replay verification.

Lena Ortega: added envelope header (digest + signer version) and a replay verification test that reconstructs sample sequences and validates digest. Added retention policy doc ref in credential-usage.md.
Jordan Blake: approve after we add an operational alert for zipper backlog growth (high backlog could mean unprocessed raw events).
Lena Ortega: added alert rule with pages for >10m backlog and dashboards for per-region backlog ratios.

lint: goreport & staticcheck — warn (unused var fixed)
source.release_notes: Security: introduce credential-usage enforcement to restrict operator tokens across jurisdictions; KMS claim translator for region-bound key selection; compact audit zipper to reduce long-term audit storage while preserving verifiable envelopes. Feature is behind a feature flag and off by default; see docs/security/credential-usage.md for rollout and migration steps.
source.testing_notes: Unit tests added for evaluator logic and translator fallbacks. Integration harness exercises residency pinning against routing and keystore proxy verification using a local test KMS emulator. Load test: end-to-end canary processed 2k req/s with translator cache TTL=30s; p99 latency delta +1.2ms. Backlog alert tuned after initial run.
source.risk_assessment: Low-medium: behavior gated and reversible. Main risk is latent performance impact from signing; mitigated with short-lived cache and optional software signing offload. Audit zipper introduces data lifecycle changes; retention policy validated with legal and compliance teams.
source.operational_playbook: 1) Feature enable via feature.flags.credential_usage_enforcer on a small subset of tenants. 2) Monitor denied_credential_usage and kms_translation_latency. 3) If denials spike unexpectedly, flip feature flag to passthrough mode and collect failing request samples. 4) For audit zipper issues, revert zipper.enable flag to stream raw events while we backfill envelopes. Runbook ID RBAC-R1.
source.notes: This PR intentionally keeps the translator signature algorithm modular (default ed25519) so HSM/KMS-backed signing can be introduced without code change. We coordinated with keystore team to accept kms_selection_proof v1 header. Legal reviewed the compact envelope format for discoverability constraints.
source.safety_signoff: Maya Chen (Security), Ravi Patel (Infra), Jordan Blake (SRE)

- [x] KMS claim translator and signed selection header
- [x] Routing pin enforcement and structured deny event
- [x] Audit zipper compression logic and retention lifecycle
- [x] Unit + integration tests for policy and translator
- [x] Migration guide and feature flag (off by default)

Compatibility/rollback: Feature gated under feature.flags.credential_usage_enforcer. Rolling back flips the gate and reverts request admission to previous path; translator has a "passthrough" mode to preserve behavior during canary. Migration notes are in docs/security/credential-usage.md.

source.title: openai-bridge: forward client RNG-hints and support structured-schema mapping for new Responses variants
source.author: Maya Chen
source.body: Summary: Add support in the OpenAI compatibility bridge for forwarding client-specified randomness hints and an explicit rng_seed field, and introduce structured-schema mapping to better handle the newer Responses API variants. This change ensures client requests that include 'randomness_hint' or 'rng_seed' are preserved through the bridge and honored by the serving runtime when possible, and maps new structured output descriptors from OpenAI-style Responses to Redwood's structured-output parser.
source.body_2: Motivation and context: The Responses family of endpoints has evolved upstream to include richer request fields (schema descriptors, function/tool-call glue metadata, and optional rng hints). Some customers expect parity between what they send to OpenAI and what we accept/forward via the compatibility layer. Previously, we dropped non-standard fields or mapped them loosely which caused reproducibility gaps and structured-output mismatches for downstream evals and tool integrations. This PR addresses three concrete gaps: 1) preserve rng-related hints so deterministic/noise-controlled workflows keep parity; 2) pass through arbitrary response.metadata keys while whitelisting known runtime fields to avoid accidental leakage; 3) add a schema_v2 mapping path for Responses variants that include typed-outputs or nested tool-call payloads.
source.body_3: What changed (high-level): - openai_adapter: accept and forward 'randomness_hint' and 'rng_seed' as top-level optional fields in compatibility translation. They become Decorator metadata on Redwood requests and are consumed by the runtime if the model/profile supports seeded sampling. - responses_adapter.py: new mapping table for schema_v2 -> structured-output format, including support for nested arrays and typed enums. - streaming_reassembly.ts: improved backpressure handling when structured chunks interleave tool-call frames; reduces duplicate reassembly when metadata frames are present. - runtime-proxy: small defensive checks to ensure passthrough metadata keys marked safe are preserved; unsafe keys are dropped with a logged audit event. - e2e/toolcall-echo.test.js: added new scenarios covering schema_v2, rng_seed propagation, and tool-call streaming order. - docs/openai-compat.md: document compatibility behaviour and examples for RNG hints and structured-schema mapping.
source.body_4: Checklist: - [x] Preserve client-sent rng_seed/randomness_hint through the bridge - [x] Map schema_v2 responses to Redwood structured format (best-effort) - [x] Add unit + integration tests for mapping and passthrough - [x] Update docs and compatibility matrix - [x] Ensure no sensitive customer headers are forwarded by default - [x] Add e2e that simulates interleaved streaming tool-call frames
source.body_5: Commits (summary): - 3f7a2d3: initial adapter plumbing and unit tests for rng hint passthrough - 9b1e4f0: add schema_v2 mapping logic + unit test cases for nested objects - 2c0b6aa: streaming reassembly improvements + flaky test fixes - a1dff42: docs & compatibility matrix update - 7e4b9d8: address review comments, tighten validation and add audit logging for dropped metadata keys
source.body_6: Files changed (high level): - services/openai_adapter/openai_adapter.go (adapter translation + metadata passthrough) - services/openai_adapter/adapter_test.go (unit tests) - integrations/responses_adapter.py (schema_v2 -> structured-output mapping) - web/streaming/streaming_reassembly.ts (reassembly/backpressure fixes) - runtime/proxy/metadata_guard.go (whitelist/deny logic and audit events) - e2e/toolcall-echo.test.js (integration scenarios) - docs/openai-compat.md (compatibility guide and examples) - sdk-tests/openai-compat-smoke.ts (smoke harness) - pkg/structured/output_parser.go (small mapping helpers)
source.body_8:
Review conversation (highlights): 
- Ethan Park (review): "Can we guarantee that x-rng-seed will not be used to create a stable path for cross-customer state? I want to ensure seeds are isolated to the request and never persisted in cache keys."
- Maya Chen (author): "Agree; added a note and enforced that Decorator seeds are only used at runtime invocation time and are never used as keys for persistent caches (see runtime/proxy/metadata_guard.go changes). Also added an audit event whenever a non-whitelisted metadata key is dropped so security can review."

- Priya Nair (review): "The schema_v2 mapping looks good, but I worry about silent failures when types mismatch. Can we surface a structured-parse warning to the response.metadata so callers can detect mapping problems?"
- Maya Chen: "Added an opt-in 'x-compat-warnings' metadata array on the response when best-effort mapping made lossy conversions. Tests updated to assert presence when expected."

- Luca Rossi (SRE): "Streaming reassembly change fixed the duplicate reassembly race we saw in staging, but this introduced a 1% higher P99 initially in canary. Did we identify cause?"
- Maya Chen: "The P99 blip was due to a defensive await added in a hot path; I reworked the backpressure logic to use a non-blocking drain and removed the await. Re-ran canary; P99 returned to baseline. Notes included in commit 2c0b6aa."
source.body_9: CI and rollout notes: - Initial CI run: lint failed (openai_adapter.go formatting + missing docstring) -> fixed in follow-up commit - Unit test suite: pass - Integration e2e: two flaky streaming tests were stabilized by the reassembly change - Canary: deployed to 'staging-region-2' with 5% traffic for 48h, observed no customer-facing errors and no incidents; small P99 blip was addressed and reverified - Rollout: merged to main and promoted to canary for dedicated clusters; feature gated by 'openai_compat_v2' flag for private installs so clusters can opt-in per-upgrade.
source.body_10: Security and privacy: We explicitly do NOT forward arbitrary headers; only a whitelisted set of metadata keys are preserved. The guard makes it easy to add entries after security review. Audit events are emitted when keys are dropped to allow security compliance verification. No customer secrets are stored or persisted by this change. The code path explicitly prevents using rng_seed values as cache keys or identifiers.

source.title: Thrift Sherpa: guided batching and quantization calibrator
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.release_notes: Introduce 'Thrift Sherpa' — an offline-guided calibrator that synthesizes workload traces to recommend per-model batching shapes and quantization presets, plus a safe gradual rollout plan. Improves unit token cost for common chat/complete workloads and provides conservative fallbacks for latency-sensitive routes.
source.description: Context: customers require lower per-token cost without sacrificing tail latency or regressions in output quality. Existing autotuners either over-aggressively quantize or recommend batch sizes that increase p95 for small requests. This PR introduces 'Thrift Sherpa', a guided calibrator that combines sampled production traces, a lightweight simulator of RTT and KV-cache hit rates, and a conservative cost model to propose batching shapes and quant profiles per model + route. Design summary: 1) Offline trace ingestion: a small trace extractor that samples token lengths, sequence distributions, and route metadata. 2) Workload simulator: fast heuristics to estimate batching throughput, p50/p95, and token-level cost under candidate batch shapes and quant profiles. 3) Scoring and constraints: multi-objective score (cost reduction weighted vs. latency SLO and quality delta budget) with hard constraints for routes tagged latency-sensitive. 4) Output: compact JSON recommendation bundle per model with ranked presets and a 'cautious' fallback. 5) Rollout helper: a manifest and a canary runner that performs step-rollouts with automatic rollback on regressions. This change touches the calibrator and the recommendations API; it does not change runtime serving kernels. Motivation: provide a middle ground between one-size-fits-all defaults and full manual profiling; enable product teams to safely adopt quantization and batching with measurable unit economics. Validation: synthetic replay of five representative customers plus two internal workloads; automated regression tests for the scoring function; A/B canary run in perf-canary for 72h with telemetry hooks. Checklist: - [x] trace extractor + schema - [x] simulator with KV/hit heuristics - [x] scoring engine and constraint enforcement - [x] recommendation manifest format and exporter - [x] unit tests and property tests for scoring bounds - [x] integration test with recommendations API - [x] perf-canary run and rollback criteria defined - [x] docs (internal guide + quickstart). Related: ENG-1182 for batching improvements, ENG-1247 for quant presets. Rollout: canary on non-critical tenants for 1 week, then staged release with telemetry gating to 0.5->1->2->5% traffic. Links: Linear ENG-1182 (design), perf-canary dashboard (internal link), design doc in docs/experimental/thrift-sherpa.md.

source.title: Emit metrics + tracing for Private backup/restore operations (with error categorization)
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.release_notes: Private deployments: backup/restore workflows now emit structured Prometheus metrics and OpenTelemetry spans, including failure categorization to power dashboards/alerts and restore drill reporting.
source.description: Motivation: SRE/on-call and customers need a consistent way to answer “are backups running?” and “why did the last restore drill fail?” across VPC + on-prem Private installs. We already log backup/restore steps in the installer, but logs alone are hard to alert on and don’t support reliable aggregation. This PR adds: (1) structured Prometheus metrics for backup + restore operations (success/failure, duration, bytes, last success timestamps), (2) OpenTelemetry tracing spans with consistent attributes for per-step visibility, and (3) explicit error categorization to drive dashboards/alerts and reduce pager noise. Scope: This instruments the control-plane backup/restore path (installer-driven + control-plane endpoints) and adds common helpers used by both “backup create” and “restore apply”. It does not change backup artifact format or encryption behavior. Metrics (Prometheus): - redwood_private_backup_runs_total{result,storage_backend,component,format_version} (counter) - redwood_private_backup_duration_seconds_bucket{result,storage_backend,component} (histogram) - redwood_private_backup_bytes_total{result,storage_backend,component} (counter) - redwood_private_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds{storage_backend,component,format_version} (gauge) - redwood_private_restore_runs_total{result,source_backend,target_env,format_version} (counter) - redwood_private_restore_duration_seconds_bucket{result,step} (histogram) - redwood_private_restore_validation_failures_total{check_name,error_category} (counter) - redwood_private_backup_restore_errors_total{operation,step,error_category} (counter; emitted on failure paths only) Tracing (OTel): Adds a top-level span per operation: “private.backup.create” and “private.restore.apply”, with child spans for steps: preflight, quiesce, snapshot, encrypt, upload, verify, bootstrap, migrate, validate. Spans include attributes: deployment_id, cluster_id (if known), namespace, storage_backend, artifact_manifest_version, backup_id/restore_id, step, result, error_category. Error categorization: Introduces a shared enum + mapping helper that classifies common failure types in a stable way for alerting/reporting. Categories include: preflight_failed, quiesce_failed, snapshot_failed, encrypt_failed, upload_failed, download_failed, verify_failed, bootstrap_failed, migrate_failed, validation_failed, compatibility_failed, permission_denied, kms_unavailable, timeout, rate_limited, unknown. Mapping is conservative: if we can’t confidently classify, we emit unknown and preserve the raw error message in logs/traces (not as a metric label) to avoid high-cardinality explosions. RBAC / permissions: No new privileges are required for the control plane, but installer pods/jobs must be allowed to emit metrics to the existing collector in Private installs. This PR reuses existing OTEL exporter wiring and the in-cluster Prometheus scrape config. Testing: - Unit tests for error classification mapping and label sanitization - Integration test in staging that runs a forced failure (bad bucket creds) and asserts metrics increments + spans have error_category - Manual validation: ran backup create / restore apply in dev VPC env; verified dashboard panels populate (see follow-up in observability-pack PR). Notes on cardinality: - No metric labels include backup_id, restore_id, customer name, or raw error strings - deployment_id is included only as a tracing attribute (not a metric label) - component label is constrained to a small set (control_plane_db, control_plane_config, k8s_manifests, audit_logs_export) - step label is constrained to known step names and used only on histogram for restore duration per step (bounded) Follow-ups: - Alert rules + dashboards land in observability-pack (PR-94) - SLO definitions tracked in slo-toolkit (PR-67) - Backup status endpoint is in PR-24318 (separate) which will consume these metrics for UI surfacing

private-installer: instrument backup create steps with counters/histograms + last_success gauge
private-installer: instrument restore apply workflow and validation checks
tracing: add otel spans for backup/restore with stable attributes and error_category
errors: add BackupRestoreErrorCategory + mapping for common failure modes
tests: unit tests for classifier + integration test for permission_denied path
docs(internal): add metrics reference + cardinality notes
conversation: Allison Grant (review): Please avoid high-cardinality labels. Specifically: do not include deployment_id as a metric label, and ensure step/component are bounded enums. Also add a short note in-code about why we don’t attach raw error strings to metrics.

Sergio Costa (author): Updated. deployment_id is trace-only now; component + step are constants with a compile-time list; raw error strings remain logs/traces only. Added comments + a label sanitizer that rejects unknown values.

Sean Gallagher (review): Can we add a metric to support paging on “no backups in X hours” without querying counters? A last_success gauge would help. Also, can restore validation failures be separated from “restore failed to run” so we can alert differently?

Sergio Costa (author): Added redwood_private_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds (gauge) emitted only on successful completion. Added restore_validation_failures_total with check_name + error_category to separate validation outcomes from workflow failures.

Ethan Park (review): Error categorization: please ensure compatibility failures (format version mismatch) are separate from generic verify_failed, and that it’s deterministic across versions. Also prefer stable names (snake_case) since SRE will write alert rules against them.
Sergio Costa (author): Added compatibility_failed and mapped version mismatch/manifest parse issues there. Enum names are snake_case and tested for stability.

Marcus Lin (review): Confirm we’re not leaking sensitive info via attributes/labels. Traces may include deployment_id; ensure that’s not customer-identifying beyond our internal UUID. Also avoid putting bucket names or KMS key IDs on spans.
Sergio Costa (author): Confirmed. No bucket names, no KMS key IDs. deployment_id is an internal UUID and only in traces. Added a denylist in attribute builder for common URL/bucket patterns.
Neha Kapoor (approval): Looks good. The last_success gauge + error_category should unblock alerting. Please make sure histogram buckets are reasonable; overly fine buckets can be noisy.
Sergio Costa (author): Buckets set to [1, 5, 10, 30, 60, 120, 300, 600, 1200, 1800] seconds for operation-level and tighter for step-level. Happy to tune post-merge based on staging data.
Allison Grant (approval): Approved with the label/cardinality changes.
