source.title: Private: expose backup status endpoints from control plane
source.author: Benji Okafor
source.release_notes: Private control plane now exposes read-only backup status endpoints (last success/attempt timestamps + error summary) for use by Console, automation, and DR readiness checks.
source.description:
Motivation: Private customers (and our own SRE/Solutions workflows) need a consistent way to surface “backup readiness” without scraping CronJob logs or inferring state from storage backends. This PR adds control-plane API endpoints that report backup status and last-success timestamps per tenant/installation, with strict authz and safe redaction (no bucket names, object keys, or secret material). This unblocks Console UI + automated restore drills to gate on “recent successful backup” and to page on backup job regressions.

What’s included: Adds a BackupStatus read model persisted by the backup orchestrator after each run (success/failure), keyed by tenant_id and environment_id; Read-only endpoints under the Private API surface; Includes timestamps (last_success_at, last_attempt_at), status enum, last_error_category + last_error_message (truncated), artifact metadata (format_version, bytes_total), and a computed “staleness” field based on configured schedule; RBAC scopes: tenant:read plus a new private:backup_status:read for installer/service automation; Optional cluster-wide endpoint for platform operators (requires internal operator role); Adds OpenAPI spec + SDK client stub used by Console; Adds audit log events for reads of backup status (low volume) and writes on backup completion.

Endpoints: GET /v1/private/tenants/{tenant_id}/backup/status -> returns latest status record for that tenant; GET /v1/private/backup/status -> operator-only list endpoint with pagination/filtering by status/staleness (intended for internal fleet dashboards; not exposed in customer docs yet).

Non-goals / out of scope: No “trigger backup” endpoint (installer/cronjob remains source of truth); No exposure of artifact locations (bucket/key) or encryption/key IDs; No attempt to infer state by reading object storage; This does not change backup/restore format.

Security + privacy notes: last_error_message is truncated to 512 chars and scrubbed via existing log redaction utility; artifact_metadata excludes backend identifiers; list endpoint is operator-only and excluded from external docs generation.

Testing: Unit tests for status model serialization, authz gates, and redaction; API integration tests covering success and failure flows; Added a contract test asserting the response schema remains backwards compatible (fields only added, never removed) to support Console; Load test sanity: list endpoint paginates and enforces max page size.

Rollout plan: Ship behind feature flag private.backupStatusEndpoints (default on for staging, off for prod) with a follow-up to enable for Private GA once Console is ready; SRE to validate via restore drill automation and dashboard wiring.

review_conversation: Ethan Park (review): Can we guarantee the status write happens even if the backup job crashes mid-run? If not, last_attempt_at could lag reality. Also please confirm where the record is stored (config DB vs separate store).

Benji Okafor (author): Write happens in the orchestrator finally{} block; for hard crashes we’ll miss last_attempt_at, but that’s true of any in-process write. I added a best-effort “attempt_start” write at the beginning to cover mid-run failures. Persisted in config DB (same as other control-plane operational metadata), table backup_status keyed by tenant_id/env_id.

Sean Gallagher (review): For on-call we need error categories to be stable for alert routing. Please avoid dumping raw exception types. Suggest enum: AUTHZ, STORAGE, ENCRYPTION, VALIDATION, UNKNOWN. Also truncate messages and scrub secrets.
Benji Okafor (author): Done. Added BackupErrorCategory enum and mapping from known failure points. Message redaction uses existing scrubber; truncated to 512 chars. Tests included.

Marcus Lin (review): Two concerns: (1) List endpoint should not be externally visible in OpenAPI docs for customers; (2) Ensure artifact metadata cannot be correlated to bucket/key (e.g., don’t return backend type + prefix). Also, audit-log reads should include actor + tenant scope.

Benji Okafor (author): Addressed. Marked operator endpoint as internal-only and excluded from public docs generation. Response includes only format_version + bytes_total; no backend identifiers. Audit event includes actor_id, authn method, tenant_id (for per-tenant endpoint) or ‘operator’ scope (for list).
Allison Grant (review): Please add a metric to track staleness distribution (or at least expose staleness_minutes as a field) so dashboards can alert on it without reimplementing schedule logic.

Benji Okafor (author): Added staleness_minutes computed server-side based on configured schedule (default 24h if unset). Also added histogram metric backup_status_staleness_minutes on reads (sampled) for internal dashboards.
Hanae Suzuki (review): What happens if schedule config is missing or misconfigured? Also confirm no KMS key IDs are returned.

Benji Okafor (author): Missing schedule -> uses 24h default and sets schedule_source=default. Misconfigured schedule -> staleness_minutes computed with default and error_category=VALIDATION set on last write. No key IDs or encryption metadata returned; only a boolean encrypted=true.
Ethan Park (approval): Looks good with the attempt_start write + authz gates. Please keep the feature flag default-off in prod until Console wiring lands.
Sean Gallagher (approval): Approved. Error categories are workable for paging/playbooks.

source.title: Smart Routing: add Hosted route canary split primitive (deterministic cohorting + region-aware enforcement)
source.author: Ravi Menon
source.description: Context: As part of Hosted canary rollouts GA work, we need a data-plane primitive that can split traffic on a Hosted route between a baseline target and a canary target by percentage, with deterministic cohort assignment (sticky per caller) and region-aware enforcement. This PR adds a new routing primitive `canary_split` to the Smart Routing evaluation + enforcement path. The primitive is intentionally minimal: it takes two compatible upstream targets (baseline + canary), a canary weight (0-100), and a deterministic cohort key, then selects the target for a request. This will be wired into the Rollout Orchestrator/control plane in follow-ups. What’s included: (1) New route config primitive `canary_split` with validation (weights must sum to 100, baseline/canary targets must be same modality and compatible model family; canary weight can be 0-100; guardrails around missing/invalid fields). (2) Deterministic cohorting: compute `cohort_bucket` = xxhash64(<cohort_key> + <route_id> + <rollout_id?>) mod 100; bucket < canary_weight => canary else baseline. If rollout_id is omitted we still include a stable config hash so cohorting does not flip on unrelated route config changes. (3) Cohort key derivation: prefer `x-redwood-cohort-key` header (set by gateway / console preview tools); fallback to API key id; final fallback to stable client IP hash when key id is unavailable (internal-only paths). We explicitly do NOT use request body content. (4) Region-aware behavior: the split is evaluated locally in each region using the same deterministic algorithm to preserve the global percentage distribution while avoiding cross-region coordination; we include region in logs but not in the hash input to avoid region-driven cohort flipping for clients that fail over. (5) Retries/streaming handling: we ensure the chosen target is pinned for the lifetime of a request (including internal retries) by storing `selected_target` in request context; for streaming requests we do not re-evaluate mid-stream. (6) Observability hooks: adds structured routing logs fields (`route_id`, `primitive=canary_split`, `cohort=baseline|canary`, `cohort_bucket`, `canary_weight`, `target_id`) and emits a low-cardinality counter `routing.canary_split.selected` with `cohort` and `region` tags only. Metric tagging with `rollout_id` is intentionally excluded here to avoid cardinality; it will be added at the gateway/telemetry layer where we can control aggregation (tracked in ENG-9152). (7) Safety: if validation fails or canary target is unhealthy/unavailable at evaluation time, we fall back to baseline and emit `routing.canary_split.fallback_to_baseline` with a reason code. Notes / decisions: - The deterministic hash input includes route_id to avoid cross-route correlation (same cohort key should not always land in canary across all routes). - We intentionally do not include region in the hash to preserve stickiness when a client fails over between regions. - This primitive does not implement auto-stop/rollback; it only selects a target. Control plane/orchestrator will adjust `canary_weight` and state transitions. Test plan: - Unit tests for validation + cohorting distribution (bucket boundaries, 0/100%, stable behavior). - Integration tests in `smart-routing-sim` to confirm selection is pinned across retries and streaming. - Manual in staging: configure a test Hosted route with 10% canary split; send requests with fixed `x-redwood-cohort-key` and observe consistent cohort selection + logs. Rollout plan: - This ships behind `routing.enable_canary_split_primitive` flag (default off). - Console/orchestrator will not emit this primitive until backend support is enabled tenant-by-tenant. Follow-ups: - Wire `rollout_id` + cohort tags end-to-end at gateway/telemetry (ENG-9152). - Console preview + validation endpoint to render the split plan (part of orchestrator/console backend PR). - Guardrail policy enforcement (max step, soak) is out of scope here (policy engine).
commits: smart-routing: add canary_split primitive schema + config validation
smart-routing: implement deterministic cohorting (xxhash64) + request context pinning
smart-routing: add region-aware enforcement + fallback to baseline on errors
obs: add routing logs fields + low-cardinality counter for cohort selection
tests: add unit/integration coverage for split selection, 0/100% edges, retry/streaming pinning
docs: add internal note on canary_split primitive and config examples
review_conversation: Mina Haddad: Please confirm whether including region in the hash input would cause cohort flips on failover. I think we should not include it.
Ravi Menon (author): Agree. I removed region from the hash input and added an explicit comment + test case for failover stickiness.
Emily Zhao: How do we ensure retries don’t bounce between baseline/canary if the gateway replays the request?
Ravi Menon (author): We pin `selected_target` in request context after first evaluation and reuse it for internal retries; added an integration test covering 2 retries with forced upstream 502.
Zachary Hill: Validation: do we need to enforce baseline/canary compatibility (same modality / model family)? Otherwise we may route chat -> embeddings accidentally.
Ravi Menon (author): Added a compatibility check in validator: both targets must have matching `target_type` and `interface` fields; rejects mismatches with a clear error.
Allison Grant: Metric cardinality: please avoid tagging with route_id/rollout_id for now; we can add cohort selection counts but keep tags bounded.
Ravi Menon (author): Done. Metrics use only `cohort` and `region`. Route/target info is in structured logs.
Ethan Park: Can we make the hash input stable across unrelated config changes? If config hash changes often, cohort assignments will churn.
Ravi Menon (author): Updated: we use route_id + (explicit rollout_id if present else `split_config_stability_key`). The stability key is derived from baseline/canary target ids + weights only.
Mina Haddad: Looks good. Please add a flag gate and default off.
Ravi Menon (author): Added `routing.enable_canary_split_primitive` with default false and documented expected enablement flow.
Mina Haddad: Approved.
Emily Zhao: Approved after retry pinning test landed.
Allison Grant: Approved.

source.title: Add trafficctl emergency GSLB weight setter for api.redwoodinference.com
source.author: Dev Patel
source.description:
### Context
During game day and recent regional brownouts, the fastest safe mitigation is to shift Hosted API traffic between regions without waiting for Terraform applies. We currently do this via manual console operations, which is error-prone.

### What this PR does
- Introduces `trafficctl`, an audited CLI wrapper around our GSLB provider API
- Adds commands to:
  - read current targets + weights
  - set weights for api.redwoodinference.com
  - set record TTL (temporary)
- Enforces RBAC via the `infra-traffic-admin` role
- Writes an audit log event for every invocation (who, what record, old/new weights)

### Usage
```
trafficctl gslb get --record api.redwoodinference.com
trafficctl dns set-ttl --record api.redwoodinference.com --ttl 60
trafficctl gslb set-weights --record api.redwoodinference.com --weights us-east-1-gw=0,us-west-2-gw=100
trafficctl dns set-ttl --record api.redwoodinference.com --ttl 300
```

### Rollback
- Disable the `trafficctl` GitHub Actions release workflow and revert to manual console operations
- No data-plane changes are required to roll back this PR

### Changed areas
- `cmd/trafficctl/*`
- `pkg/gslb/*`
- `pkg/auditlog/*`
- `rbac/policies/infra-traffic-admin.yaml`
- `docs/runbooks/global-traffic-routing-controls.md` (synced to Confluence by docs bot)

### Review notes
- Hannah: please verify audit event fields match incident review needs
- Alex: verify gateway target naming conventions (us-east-1-gw, us-west-2-gw)

### Review conversation (excerpt)
Hannah Schmitt: Can we ensure we log *previous weights* as well as the new weights? That's critical for rollback during an incident.

Dev Patel: Yep, updated `auditlog.Event` to include `old_weights` and `new_weights` as strings (serialized).

Ethan Park: Any safety rails to prevent accidental 100% to an unknown target?

Dev Patel: Added validation: target must be in the allowlist for the record; otherwise command aborts.

Alex Martinez: Looks good. Please add a short note that TTL should be restored after the cutover.

Dev Patel: Added to docs + examples.

### Merge
- Squash merged into main
- CI: build/test/lint passed
source.release_notes: Internal: `trafficctl` CLI for emergency Hosted API cross-region traffic cutover (audited + RBAC guarded).

- Adds commands to:
  - read current targets + weights
  - set weights for api.redwoodinference.com
  - set record TTL (temporary)
- Enforces RBAC via the `infra-traffic-admin` role
- Writes an audit log event for every invocation (who, what record, old/new weights)

### Usage
```
trafficctl gslb get --record api.redwoodinference.com
trafficctl dns set-ttl --record api.redwoodinference.com --ttl 60
trafficctl gslb set-weights --record api.redwoodinference.com --weights us-east-1-gw=0,us-west-2-gw=100
trafficctl dns set-ttl --record api.redwoodinference.com --ttl 300
```

### Rollback
- Disable the `trafficctl` GitHub Actions release workflow and revert to manual console operations
- No data-plane changes are required to roll back this PR

### Changed areas
- `cmd/trafficctl/*`
- `pkg/gslb/*`
- `pkg/auditlog/*`
- `rbac/policies/infra-traffic-admin.yaml`
- `docs/runbooks/global-traffic-routing-controls.md` (synced to Confluence by docs bot)

### Review notes
- Hannah: please verify audit event fields match incident review needs
- Alex: verify gateway target naming conventions (us-east-1-gw, us-west-2-gw)

### Review conversation (excerpt)
Hannah Schmitt: Can we ensure we log *previous weights* as well as the new weights? That's critical for rollback during an incident.

Dev Patel: Yep, updated `auditlog.Event` to include `old_weights` and `new_weights` as strings (serialized).

Ethan Park: Any safety rails to prevent accidental 100% to an unknown target?

Dev Patel: Added validation: target must be in the allowlist for the record; otherwise command aborts.

Alex Martinez: Looks good. Please add a short note that TTL should be restored after the cutover.

Dev Patel: Added to docs + examples.

- Squash merged into main
- CI: build/test/lint passed

source.title: openai-invoke-affinity-annotations-and-fragment-serializer
source.author: Maya Park
source.description: Motivation: Improve OpenAI-compat tool/function-call selection by exposing an 'invoke_affinity' annotation on tool metadata and by making streaming fragments self-descriptive so tool calls are never mis-routed or partially applied by downstream coalescers. Summary: 1) Add a non-breaking 'invoke_affinity' optional annotation to the tool registry and mapper (values: high|normal|low|prefer_local) that is used by the OpenAI-compat selection heuristic to bias tool_choice without hard overrides. 2) Serialize tool invocation fragments emitted during streaming with a compact header (v1) containing id, affinity, and schema-hash so fragment coalescers and proxies can re-assemble or route fragments deterministically. 3) Update the compatibility layer to treat tool messages with affinity=high as preferred targets for low-latency lanes (dedicated pools) and affinity=prefer_local to prefer the caller's region. 4) Ensure backwards compatibility: missing invoke_affinity behaves the same as 'normal' and fragment header is optional but validated when present. Checklist: - [x] design doc + API sketch included in PR description - [x] runtime mapper + serializer implementation - [x] unit + integration tests for selection weighting and streaming reassembly - [x] docs and SDK TS surface updates - [x] migration notes and rollout plan - [x] perf microbench showing ~0.9x selector CPU and no additional p99 token latency Changes are scoped so the OpenAI-compat surface preserves the existing function calling contract while giving product teams a low-risk knob to bias routing for latency/cost. See linked Linear tickets and end-to-end test results. Migration: rollout via feature flag 'openai.invoke_affinity' for 2 weeks, collect telemetry; revert safe if any metric regressions >5% on request success or latency. Backwards compatibility: older proxies that ignore the fragment header will still receive integrity-protected JSON tool payloads; routers that see affinity will incorporate it into their weighted selection but will not change function signatures. Related: this complements the 'stream-fragment-tagging' work but focuses on tool selection semantics rather than coalescing heuristics.
commits: a1d2f3c: add invoke_affinity field to tool metadata and mapper
b3e4f5a: implement compact fragment header serializer/deserializer
c6d7e8b: integrate affinity into openai compatibility selection heuristic
d9f0a1b: add unit tests for weighting and header roundtrip
e2f3a4c: docs: describe invoke_affinity and migration plan
f5b6c7d: sdk(ts): surface affinity on ToolDescriptor and examples
diff_snippets:
services/openai-compat/src/tool_mapper.rs: +pub enum InvokeAffinity { High, Normal, Low, PreferLocal }
+impl Default for InvokeAffinity { fn default() -> Self { InvokeAffinity::Normal } }

services/serving/runtime/stream_serializer.rs: +// v1 header: 1 byte version | 1 byte flags | 16 byte tool-id | var affinity-tag | 4 byte schema-hash
+fn write_fragment_header(w: &mut impl Write, hdr: &FragmentHeader) -> io::Result<()> { /* compact encoding */ }

sdk/typescript/src/tool_descriptor.ts: +export type InvokeAffinity = 'high' | 'normal' | 'low' | 'prefer_local'
+export interface ToolDescriptor { id: string; name: string; invokeAffinity?: InvokeAffinity }
review_comments: Ethan Liu: Quick note — why are we using 'prefer_local' instead of a region tag? Can we expand the enum later without schema churn?

Maya Park (author): We chose 'prefer_local' as a lightweight hint; it's intentionally orthogonal to explicit region tags so schedulers can use both. We'll document extension rules in the compatibility doc.
Priya Natarajan: Integration test failed once on flaky network emulation, re-run CI after addressing stream backpressure edge-case.
Maya Park (author): Fixed backpressure by deferring header emission until first non-empty fragment; re-ran tests and added a stress case.
source.release_notes: Introduce optional invoke_affinity annotation for tool metadata and a streaming fragment header v1 to improve deterministic routing and avoid partial tool-call application during streaming. Backwards compatible; rollout behind feature flag.
source.notes: Rollout plan: enable flag on 10% of traffic for 48h, inspect selector-weighted telemetry, then 100% if no regressions. SDKs publish minor bump. Ops: update routing policy to read affinity hints but ensure cost guardrails are enforced.

source.title: catalog: surface model capability flags, pricing and limits for OpenAI-compat clients
source.author: Maya Patel
ci_checks: unit-tests: initially failed (kv-cache flake) -> rerun -> pass
source.description:
Motivation: Clients using the OpenAI-compat layer and our public model listing need a single, predictable surface to discover per-model capabilities (streaming stability, function/tool-calling support, structured-output specializations) and unit economics (pricing per token, soft/hard limits and concurrency). This PR extends the model catalog API and internal catalog objects to include capability and pricing metadata, plus server-side filters that power the console and SDKs.

Summary of changes: added explicit capability flags (openai_compat, supports_streaming_stable, supports_function_calling, structured_output_profiles) to the model listing; exposed priced_units and limits metadata (cost_per_input_token, cost_per_output_token, max_tokens_per_request, recommended_concurrency, rate_limit_tier). Implemented new query params for the GET /v1/models endpoint to filter by capability, max_estimated_cost_per_k, min_stability_score, region, and quantization support. Updated the OpenAI compatibility layer to prefer models with openai_compat=true for compatibility endpoints and to fall back predictably to compatible variants when a requested capability is not available. Added docs, SDK bindings, and tests.

Why this is safe: fields are additive. No breaking schema changes for existing consumers. Default behavior for compatibility endpoints is unchanged unless clients explicitly query the new fields or filtering. We included a migration path for older model entries missing cost metadata (defaults used and surfaced as "unknown").

API additions (examples):
- GET /v1/models?capability=function_calling&region=us-east-1&max_estimated_cost_per_k=0.50 -> returns models that advertise function calling and whose cost_per_1k_tokens <= 0.50.
- New model-listing response includes keys: capabilities (list), pricing (object), limits (object), compatibility_flags (list). Example snippet in response body (condensed for PR):
  "models": [{"id": "redwood-slim-1b", "capabilities": ["embeddings","streaming"], "pricing": {"cost_per_input_token": "0.00005", "cost_per_output_token": "0.00006", "currency": "USD"}, "limits": {"max_tokens": "4096", "recommended_concurrency": "4"}, "compatibility_flags": ["openai_compat"]}]

Implementation notes:
- internal/catalog/store: extended ModelRecord with flattened string lists for capabilities and compatibility_flags; pricing/limits stored as strings to avoid schema churn in metadata blobs.
- api/handlers/models: added parsing for new query params and server-side filter combinators; implemented pagination and estimation of cost-per-request for the max_estimated_cost_per_k filter.
- openai-compat layer: updated selection logic to prefer exact capability matches, then compatibility-flagged fallbacks, then nearest-latency alternative. This avoids regression for streaming clients while giving a predictable fallback path.
- SDK updates: python and ts SDKs gain ModelCatalog.list_models() optional filters.
- docs: new reference doc with examples and migration guidance.

Testing: Added unit tests for filter logic, integration tests exercising the openai-compat routing, and a small perf smoke to ensure the filter path does not add >1ms median overhead. CI initially caught a flaky kv-cache test unrelated to the change; rerun resolved it.

Rollout plan: Feature is behind a feature flag (model_catalog_capabilities_v2) and enabled for internal and canary tenants first. Console uses the new fields only when the flag is on. After 2 weeks of canary telemetry (stability and no client errors), we will flip default and update external SDK releases.

Related: ENG-4821 (catalog UX for capability discovery), SUP-188 (customer reported mismatch between advertised function calling and actual routing), docs PR will follow to expand examples.

Notes for reviewers: focus on the filter combinator correctness (api/handlers/models/filter.go), openai-compat selection priority (pkg/openai/selector.go) and the SDK bindings. The JSON snippet above is illustrative; exact field names are in the updated API doc file.
commits: c8f7a4a: api: extend model listing response with capability and pricing metadata
9b3e2f1: internal: persist pricing/limits as strings and add migration defaults
412d7b0: openai-compat: selection improvements to prefer compatibility_flag matches
5f1e2c9: sdk: add list_models filters to python and typescript clients
a21b6f4: docs: model-listing reference and migration notes
d4ee0d5: tests: add unit and integration coverage for filter logic
review_conversation: Ava Chen: Could we keep pricing fields optional in the public response if unknown? Some older models don't have pricing metadata. Maya Patel: Yes — we return 'unknown' strings for cost fields when missing and documented in migration notes.
Diego Martinez: The selector changes look good; can we add a tie-breaker by recommended_concurrency? PRiya Nair: Added tie-breaker using recommended_concurrency then latency; updated tests.
Liam O'Connor: Integration tests look flaky locally for one case — fails when the canary tenant doesn't have any openai_compat models. Maya Patel: Added a canary fixture in the test harness; reran.
Ava Chen: Approved after addressing docs and ensuring feature flag gating is in place.
source.release_notes: Expose model capabilities and pricing in the model listing. New filters for capability, region, quantization, and estimated cost per 1k tokens. Additive API change; default behavior unchanged. Clients should opt into new fields via filters or SDK updates.

source.title: Introduce probabilistic span sampler and kernel branch-counter hooks for adaptive scheduler insights
source.author: Ava Chen
source.release_notes: Adds a low-overhead probabilistic span sampler (weighted by KV-hit latency and batch position) and new kernel branch/mispredict counters exported to the perf dashboard. No API changes. Improves root-cause visibility for adaptive scheduling regressions.
commits: a1f3c9c: perf: add weighted span sampler core and sampler config
b4e8d2f: runtime: expose kernel branch counters to tracing backend
c2d7f13: infra: perf-dashboard mapping and query panels
d9b4a5e: tests: add microbench harness and CI test for sampler determinism
e7f1b02: docs: sampler config and dashboard guide
source.description:
Context and motivation: We see intermittent scheduler degradation for long-tail requests where KV cache hits are correlated with kernel branch behavior and micro-stalls. Existing NVTX and epoch counters capture timing but not branch-level dynamics. This PR introduces a probabilistic (weighted) span sampler that biases capture toward requests with high KV lookup latency and late-batch positions, plus a small set of kernel branch/mispredict counters exposed through the telemetry pipeline so dashboard panels can correlate scheduler decisions with low-level CPU/GPU branch behavior.

What this PR does (single-string description): Adds a lightweight probabilistic span sampler (runtime/sampling/weighted_span_sampler.{cc,h}) that samples at enqueue time based on configurable weights: kv_hit_latency (ms), batch_index, and recent_retry_count. The sampler emits full NVTX-range + span payload for sampled requests only, keeping steady-state overhead near 0.3% at 95th percentile load in our microbench. Introduces kernel branch counters (telemetry/kernel_branch_counters.cpp) that aggregate branch-mispredict and branch-instruction counts at the scheduler worker boundary; counters are aggregated per-epoch and batched to the existing telemetry pipeline. Updates perf-dashboard json (observability/dashboards/weighted-span-sampler.json) with panels that join span traces and branch counters, plus a dedicated query to highlight high-latency KV lookups with elevated branch-mispredict ratios. Includes unit tests and an integration microbench (bench/micro/sampler_adaptive_sched_bench.cc) that reproduces regression patterns.

Checklist (author): - [x] Implementation of weighted span sampler core - [x] Kernel branch counters and telemetry export - [x] Dashboard panels and query snippets - [x] Deterministic microbench and unit tests - [x] Docs for ops on how to interpret panels and sampler tuning

Changed areas (high-level file list): runtime/sampling/weighted_span_sampler.cc, runtime/sampling/weighted_span_sampler.h, telemetry/kernel_branch_counters.cpp, telemetry/kernel_branch_counters.h, observability/dashboards/weighted-span-sampler.json, bench/micro/sampler_adaptive_sched_bench.cc, tests/sampling/test_weighted_sampler.cpp, docs/observability/sampler.md, BUILD.bazel (telemetry exports).

Test/CI notes: CI performed three runs. First run failed a flaky perf-test (bench micro reliability) due to environmental jitter; second run re-ran the flaky job and passed. All unit tests and integration tests pass locally and in CI. End-to-end telemetry e2e smoke was validated in staging with a synthetic traffic pattern; dashboard queries returned expected joins.

Review conversation (selected highlights):
- Diego Martins (review): "Nice approach — weighting by KV hit latency makes sense. Two asks: (1) make the sampler weight function pluggable (so ops can try other heuristics), and (2) please add an opt-out runtime flag for customers concerned about any additional counters."
- Ava Chen (author reply): "Pluggable weight function is implemented via a SamplerStrategy interface (see sampling/strategy.h); default strategy remains the weighted heuristic. Added runtime flag --telemetry.disable_branch_counters with documentation and feature-gate check."
- Priya Patel (review): "Telemetry export looks good; can you add a dashboard note explaining expected baseline ranges for branch-mispredict ratio? Otherwise panels are noisy for new tenants."
- Ava Chen: "Added doc section with baseline guidance and a small script to compute baseline from a 1-hour sample."
- Marcus Lee (approval): "+1 after tests re-run — please squash on merge to keep history tidy."

Performance measurements: Microbench in bench/micro shows sampler overhead <0.5% p95 request CPU and memory delta in a 64-core staging cluster; sampling rate default is 0.1% (configurable). Branch counters add ~0.2% overhead at aggregation boundary when enabled. Combined telemetry ingestion size increased by ~0.8% at default sampling rate. Long-tail test: when enabling weighted sampling, traces for 99.9th percentile latency requests increased by ~18x (samples) enabling better RCA visibility.

Operational notes and config: Default config retains existing telemetry costs: sampler default rate 0.001 (0.1%), weight_exponent 1.2, kv_latency_threshold_ms 1.0. Operators can change strategy via telemetry.sampler.strategy=<name> and disable branch counters via telemetry.disable_branch_counters=true. Dashboard docs include PromQL/ESQL examples to compute mispredict_ratio = branch_mispredicts / branch_instructions.

Potential risks and mitigation: Branch counters require a small kernel shim for CPUs on some older distros — guarded behind feature gate and disabled by default. We instrument aggregation carefully to avoid contention (batch buffer + single flush per-epoch). If customers enable aggressive sampling, telemetry costs can increase; guidance added to docs.

Merge notes: Squash merge performed by Marcus Lee after CI re-run and approvals. Changelog entry added under Observability / Telemetry.

Related work and follow-ups: Follow-up tickets created to (1) explore GPU-side branch sampling equivalents (ENG-3532) and (2) a background replayer that uses sampled spans to auto-propose scheduler policy tweaks (ENG-3551).
source.notes_for_release: Observability: probabilistic weighted span sampling and kernel branch counters added. Default disabled kernel counters; sampler is on at a low default rate. See docs/observability/sampler.md for tuning guidance and dashboard links.

source.title: Hybrid quant execution paths: FP8-aware kernels, INT8 dequant+matmul fusion and scheduler refactor
source.author: Ava Chen
source.release_notes: Introduces FP8-aware matmul kernels and an INT8 dequant+matmul fusion path; refactors request scheduler to expose adaptive batch windows and preemptive slotting. Expected performance: 1.8-2.6x throughput on mixed-precision workloads and 10-20% tail latency reduction for common chat workloads.
source.description: Motivation: customers running large conversational workloads push two axes of optimization simultaneously: cheaper quantized kernels (INT8/FP8) and smarter scheduling to preserve latency SLOs when batching. This PR implements a hybrid execution strategy that (1) adds FP8-aware matmul kernels with per-channel scale metadata, (2) implements an INT8 dequant+matmul fusion execution path to avoid intermediate FP buffers when upstream weights are INT8 and downstream ops permit FP accumulation, and (3) refactors the request scheduler to expose an adaptive batch window and preemptive slotting to reduce head-of-line and tail latency during bursty traffic. Summary of changes: new kernel files under serving/runtime/kernels/{fp8_matmul.cc,fp8_matmul.h,quant_fusion.cc}, changes to kernel registry to prefer fused path when data layout and accumulation type are compatible, scheduler refactor in serving/runtime/scheduler/{adaptive_window.rs,slotting.rs} with a conservative default policy that preserves existing latency behavior, KV cache interaction updates in serving/runtime/cache to bypass copy-on-dequant for fused paths, continuous batching adjustments to prefer short windows for chat-like traffic and longer windows for bulk embed workloads, micro-bench harness and bench scripts added to infra/bench/quant_hybrid. Commits (high-level): 1) initial FP8 kernel implementation and microbench harness, 2) INT8 dequant+matmul fusion path and kernel registry integration, 3) scheduler refactor and policy configuration knobs, 4) KV cache bypass and tests, 5) docs + benchmarks + small followups. Files changed (high level): serving/runtime/kernels/* (new kernels and tests), serving/runtime/registry.cc (selection logic), serving/runtime/scheduler/* (refactor and policy), serving/runtime/cache/* (KV cache bypass), infra/bench/* (bench scripts), docs/perf/quant-hybrid.md (guidance and numbers), tests/unit/* (numerics and scheduler unit tests). Benchmarks: end-to-end microbench on a T4/80GB-equivalent collector shows FP8 matmul 1.8-2.1x faster than FP16 baseline for long sequences and 2.3-2.6x for short dense matmuls; INT8 fused path reduced memory pressure and improved throughput by ~1.4x in mixed workloads. Tail latency (p95-p99) improved by 10-20% across our chat-like workload mix when the adaptive batch window is enabled with default conservative params. Cost projection: for a typical chat endpoint these changes reduce per-token GPU cycles by ~18% at our sampled workload, with larger wins for model families that compress well to FP8. Testing and CI: added unit tests for numeric parity (relative error tracking with random seeds), integration tests for fused path selection, and scheduler unit tests with simulated arrival patterns. CI initially showed flakiness on scheduler timing tests due to coarse clocks in the test harness; tests stabilized after tightening timing expectations and adding deterministic seeds. Review conversation highlights: Liam O'Connor: "Numerical stability seems OK on the sampled seeds but please add a stress test with longer sequences and underflow/overflow envelopes." Author (Ava Chen): "Added stress test harness and long-sequence seeds, documented the worst-case relative error and safe fallbacks to FP16 accumulation in the registry when scale overflow is detected." Grace Patel: "Scheduler changes look good; can we make the adaptive window configurable per-route?" Author: "Yes, added per-route knobs with sane defaults and a global override for Dedicated customers." Rohit Mehra: "Bench numbers look promising; please add scripts to reproduce and commit summaries of raw results." Author: "Bench scripts committed to infra/bench with a README pointing to s3 artifact locations; raw CSVs attached to Linear ticket ENG-5821." CI & approvals: initial CI run failed on flaky timing test, second run passed; reviewers Grace and Rohit approved after perf/regression signoff; Liam gave an approve-after-changes tag once stress tests added. Migration and config: new config flags added to runtime config schema: quant.hybrid.enabled, quant.hybrid.strict_scale_check, scheduler.adaptive_window.enabled, scheduler.adaptive_window.max_ms. Default values preserve previous behavior unless quant.hybrid.enabled is set. Backwards compatibility: the fused path is opt-in via registry selection and falls back to legacy dequant path on numeric anomalies; no customer-facing API changes. Documentation: docs/perf/quant-hybrid.md contains recommendations on when to enable FP8/INT8 paths, how to tune scheduler knobs for chat vs embed workloads, and an appendix with reproduced bench numbers and command lines. Follow-ups: 1) larger FP8 coverage for other kernels (conv1d, rnn) tracked in ENG-5830, 2) investigate hardware-specific low-level kernels for A100/A800 in a follow-up PR, 3) expand regression testing to include stochastic load patterns in canary. Merge: squashed and merged to main on 2024-11-21 after approvals and green CI. Artifacts: bench outputs and CSVs uploaded to s3://redwood-ci/bench/pr-31207/.

source.title: Fork-and-Join rollout controller with stable session affinity
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Introduce ForkJoinRollout CRD and controller to enable parallel path rollouts with deterministic session affinity and safe join semantics. Includes metrics gates and rollback tracing.
source.description:
Summary: Introduce a new Fork-and-Join rollout controller and CRD (ForkJoinRollout) that allows operators to split traffic into multiple parallel rollout paths (forks), run independent validation windows, then atomically join a winning path back into the primary serving graph while preserving stable session affinity and KV cache locality. Motivation: Some experiments require running two or more independent model variants for overlapping cohorts and then merging the best-performing variant into production without user-visible churn or cache thrash. Existing canary and shadow primitives support linear progressive rollouts but do not provide first-class semantics for forked experiments and deterministic joins. This PR implements the control plane and runtime hooks to enable fork-and-join workflows with safety fences and rollback tracebacks. Changes: Commits: 1) Add CRD and API surface for ForkJoinRollout with fields: forks[], joinPolicy{windowMs, quorum, metricsGates}, affinity{cookieHeader, hashSalt}, rollbackTraceTTL 2) Implement controller reconciliation loop: deterministic cohort hashing, fork assignment, per-fork observation windows, metric aggregation, join decision, and transactional switch-over. 3) Runtime integration: routing plugin that honors session affinity cookie/header to route existing sessions consistently to their assigned fork across restarts. 4) KV cache migration helpers that pin or warm keys for transitioning cohorts to avoid cache cold-start spikes at join. 5) Metrics and alerting: new rollout.* metrics, join_decision_latency, affinity_mismatch_count, cache_warmup_effect. 6) E2E tests: simulated traffic harness, chaos test (controller restarts), and load test to validate no-traffic-glitch join. Files changed (high-level):
- k8s/crds/forkjoinrollout.yaml: new CRD and schema.
- controllers/forkjoin/controller.go: main reconciliation implementation and state machine.
- controllers/forkjoin/affinity.go: deterministic hashing and cookie utility.
- runtime/router/plugin_forkjoin.go: router plugin that preserves affinity and implements transactional join.
- runtime/cache/warmup.go: helpers to migrate/warm keys during join.
- observability/metrics_rollouts.prom: new metrics.
- tests/e2e/forkjoin/harness.go: traffic harness and validators.
- docs/deployments/forkjoin.md: operator guide and examples.
- scripts/bench/forkjoin_load_test.sh: benchmark harness configs.
- go.mod/go.sum and small refactors in existing rollout packages.
Review thread summary:
- Ravi: "Controller looks solid but we need stronger invariants around rollbackTraceTTL to avoid orphaned sessions." Author (Maya): "Added TTL enforcement in reconciliation and a periodic sweep task; documented behaviour."
- Sofia: "Affinity cookie name collisions with other RBAC cookies are possible in some proxies." Author: "Namespace-prefixed cookie names and a proxy integration guideline added to docs."
- Ethan: "Will join be atomic from a client perspective? What about in-flight KV cache updates?" Author: "Join is transactional at routing layer: existing sessions continue hitting assigned variant until their TTL expires; new sessions post-join get routed to joined variant. KV warmup reduces cold-start; we added a brief overlap window where both variants are served to smooth cache state."
Requested changes and iterations:
- Added unit tests for hashing function and cookie header fallbacks.
- Added additional metrics for debugging affinity mismatches.
- Tightened concurrency handling in controller reconciliation to avoid double-assigning cohorts under controller duplication.
CI: Initial CI run flagged a flaky e2e test on the harness timeout.
Fixes: increased harness timeout and added retries for the chaos step.
Final CI status: all checks passed on rerun (unit, integration, e2e, lint, go vet).
Performance validation: Microbenchmarks show median routing latency increase of <0.6ms when affinity cookie is present. Load tests demonstrate join window traffic spike mitigated by KV warmups in 95th percentile CPU usage.
Migration notes:
- The new CRD is opt-in; cluster admins must apply k8s/crds/forkjoinrollout.yaml.
- Default behavior is disabled unless a ForkJoinRollout object is created.
- Operators should configure affinity.hashSalt to ensure deterministic cohort assignment across controller upgrades.
Rollback behavior:
- The controller records a rollback trace when a fork fails metrics gates; traces are retained for rollbackTraceTTL and can be used to replay traffic for debugging.
- Automatic rollback will reroute new sessions away from the failing fork and optionally quarantine traffic via shadowing for post-mortem.
Backwards compatibility and safety:
- No changes to existing Canary or TrafficSplit CRDs.
- Router plugin is feature-flagged; when disabled, the routing falls back to prior routing semantics.
- CRD schema includes strict validation to prevent malformed rollouts.
Example usage: see docs/deployments/forkjoin.md for YAML samples demonstrating a 2-fork A/B-like experiment where traffic is split by hashed user_id, independent metrics gates are applied, and the controller performs a join after both forks exceed thresholds.
Merge: Squash-merged to main after approvals from reviewers and green CI.
Related work: complements the A/B and canary toolset and provides a safer path for multi-variant experiments that require atomic join semantics.
Linear: ENG-4210 (Design and API), ENG-4278 (Runtime router & cache warmup).
Notes for on-call: If affinity_mismatch_count spikes post-deploy, roll forward with controller disable flag to revert to legacy routing, or apply a focused CR to adjust affinity.hashSalt. This PR is intentionally conservative in defaults and feature-flagged to allow incremental rollout.

- Added unit tests for hashing function and cookie header fallbacks.
- Added additional metrics for debugging affinity mismatches.

Motivation: Some experiments require running two or more independent model variants for overlapping cohorts and then merging the best-performing variant into production without user-visible churn or cache thrash. Existing canary and shadow primitives support linear progressive rollouts but do not provide first-class semantics for forked experiments and deterministic joins. This PR implements the control plane and runtime hooks to enable fork-and-join workflows with safety fences and rollback tracebacks. source.title: streaming-reconstruction-guard-and-toolcall-stable-handback
source.author: Ava Chen
release_notes: Fix partial-JSON reconstruction when SSE fragments split inside escaped sequences
Introduce reconstruction guard that prevents tool/function-call reorderings during stream replay
Improve OpenAI-compat layer to surface handback-fidelity metadata for client heuristics
source.description: Motivation: We observed a class of in-production incidents where SSE fragment splits inside escaped JSON strings or multi-byte UTF-8 sequences could cause downstream consumers to see re-ordered function/tool call boundaries or emit malformed partial JSON. This PR introduces a lightweight runtime 'reconstruction guard' that assigns per-fragment fidelity credits and a deterministic handback protocol to ensure toolcall boundaries remain ordered across retransmit/replay and partial-frame repair. Summary of changes: 1) runtime/streaming: add FragmentFidelityLedger and a reconstruction guard that tracks credit windows and stalls handback until a safe boundary is observed or the ledger times out; 2) sse/parser: introduce escape-aware tail rewriter to avoid splitting inside escapes and normalize trailing delimiters before emission; 3) compat/openai: emit X-Redwood-Handback-Fidelity header and annotate stream events with handback_id so OpenAI-compat clients can prefer resumable handoff points; 4) protocol: deterministic toolcall handoff algorithm that converts ephemeral function-call tokens into ordered handback checkpoints to prevent interleaving when fragments arrive out-of-order; 5) tests: large suite of synthetic fragment replays and property tests that simulate jittered, coalesced, and reordered chunks. Implementation notes: The reconstruction guard uses a small in-memory ledger per connection (bounded to 32 entries) and a sliding credit window to avoid unbounded buffering. When ledger credit is insufficient, the connection flushes an escape-normalized delimiter and advances a handback checkpoint; this behavior preserves low-latency in common cases while preventing rare misorders. The OpenAI-compat annotations are backward compatible: older clients ignore handback_id and continue to operate normally. Checklist: - [x] Core runtime changes implemented and covered by unit tests - [x] End-to-end tests with python and ts SDKs - [x] Backwards compatibility validated against existing OpenAI-compat integration tests - [x] Performance microbenchmarks show <0.6% median latency regression on p4d for synthetic workloads - [x] Feature flagged behind runtime.feature.streaming_reconstruct_guard rollout plan: feature off in production initial cut; enable for 10% traffic with canary instrumentation and latency/error dashboards; expand to 100% after 48h with no regressions. Related: ENG-2407 describes incident notes and replay corpus. This description includes small illustrative pseudo-patch: "if (fragment.endsWith('\\') || insideEscape(fragment)) normalizeEscapeTail(fragment)" which is implemented in sse/parser/tail_rewrite.go.
commits: a9f4d2c stabilize: add fidelity ledger and eviction policy
b3e1a7f compat: attach handback_id header and event annotation
c7d3b21 parser: escape-aware tail rewriter and delimiter normalizer
d2f6e09 runtime: reconstruction guard integration and config flag
e8a5f33 tests: fragment replay property suite and sdk e2e harness
source.review_conversation: Mateo Ruiz: Please explain how the ledger bound interacts with long-running streams; are we at risk of dropping fidelity for very chatty sessions? Ava Chen: Ledger is per-connection and bounded to 32 entries by default; for chatty sessions we prefer more frequent handbacks so credit churn is lower rather than unbounded buffering. Janelle Park: Can you show the perf delta on p3/p4? Ava Chen: Microbenchmarks included, median latency regressions are <0.6% on p4d and tail changes are within noise. Samuel Becker: Add a unit test for splitting inside a 4-byte UTF8 codepoint. Ava Chen: Added property test that injects multibyte boundary splits and verifies JSON reassembly and call order. Mateo Ruiz: Approved. Janelle Park: Approved. Samuel Becker: Approved after requested test added.
source.ci_checks: ci/build: pass; ci/unit: pass; ci/integration: pass; ci/e2e-python: pass; ci/e2e-ts: pass; ci/perf-smoke: pass (rerun once to stabilize flakey network harness)
source.merge_outcome: merged via squash on 2024-11-20 by Ava Chen. Merge commit summary: squash: streaming reconstruction guard + compat annotations + tests
source.notes_for_ops: Feature gated behind runtime.feature.streaming_reconstruct_guard. To roll back disable the flag in the feature service and restart stream-workers in a staged fashion. If misordering symptoms appear, enable compat/openai.vet_handback_checks and consult the fragment replay logs (tag: fragment-replay).

source.title: delta-driven pin throttling and tiered reuse allocator for paged KV
source.author: Sofia Patel
source.release_notes: Introduce delta-driven pin throttling and a tiered reuse pool for paged KV; reduces tail latency and lowers memory churn under mixed workloads.
source.description: Motivation: Our paged KV block manager saw high tail latency and frequent pin-induced stalls when conversational workloads produce short bursts of focused hot prefixes. Existing heuristics pinned aggressively on first-detect hotness and coalesced eagerly, which increased contention and caused kernel scheduling jitter. This change introduces a delta-driven pin heuristic plus a lightweight tiered reuse allocator to smooth pin activity and defer expensive coalesces into the background.
source.detailed_changes:
- Delta-driven pin throttle: compute a per-span "delta" metric = recent_access_rate - historic_access_rate; only escalate pin priority when delta crosses a configurable hysteresis threshold. This prevents immediate hard pins for transient bursts and reduces pin churn.
- Tiered reuse allocator: hot/warm/cold lanes for freed blocks. Blocks evicted into warm can be rehydrated quickly without full reallocation; cold lane is low-priority and compacted in background. The allocator uses a compact epoch-based reclamation to avoid locking hot paths.
- Deferred coalesce worker: coalesce and merge adjacent free spans in a low-priority background worker; opportunistic coalesces happen inline only when contention is low.
- Pin throttling backpressure: when overall pin rate exceeds budget we throttle new high-priority pins and apply an exponential backoff per-request to avoid head-of-line stalls.
- Scheduler hints: the runtime's request router receives a lightweight hint about expected hot-span locality (hot/warm/cold) so continuous-batcher can favor batches that avoid heavily pinned spans and the kernel selector can choose low-latency kernels when locality is high.
- Tests and perf harness: added unit tests for delta metric, integration tests for epoch reclamation, new stress profile in perf-canary that simulates rapid conversational prefix changes, and updated observability to expose delta, pin_budget, and tier occupancy metrics.
commits: a1f3c9f: introduce delta metric and hysteresis config
b7e2d4a: implement tiered reuse allocator (hot/warm/cold lanes)
c9d8b2f: add deferred coalesce worker and epoch reclamation
d4f7e3a: expose pin budget and tier occupancy metrics to console
e2a9b0c: integrate scheduler hints into continuous batcher
f5c1a77: tests: unit + integration + perf-canary scenario
g3b4f11: fix lint and type issues, adjust docstrings
source.size_and_rationale: Moderate-sized change focused on hot path performance. We avoid heavyweight locks on allocation hot paths by pushing reclamation and coalesce into background epochs. The observable improvements came from smoothing pin events and reducing IO spikes during bursts.
source.perf_testing_summary: Perf canary (mixed chat, 32-client, variable prefix churn): p50 throughput +9%, p95 latency -12%, p99 latency -18% on average across 3 runs. Memory churn (alloc/free cycles) reduced ~22% due to warm-lane reuse. On a synthetic write-heavy workload the change had neutral throughput but lowered tail latency by ~5%.
source.ci_history: Initial CI: failed on lint (header ordering) and a flakey allocator test under TSAN. After fixes: rerun CI passed all presubmit checks and the perf-canary triggered successfully in staging. One stress run in CI revealed a race in deferred coalesce; added a lightweight epoch barrier to fix it.
review_comments: Aaron Chen: Nice approach. Can you quantify the extra memory footprint for warm-lane staging? Also ensure the default hysteresis isn't too aggressive for low-QPS customers.

Sofia Patel (author): Warm-lane adds a small per-span tag and a freelist pointer; measured at ~0.8% memory overhead in our canary. Hysteresis default tuned from perf runs; added config and notes in RFC.
Priya Natarajan: How does this interact with dedicated capacity autoscaler? If pins are throttled, will we cascade to scaling decisions?

Sofia Patel: We emit pin_budget and pin_throttle_ratio metrics. Autoscaler policy in Dedicated can factor pin_throttle_ratio as a signal; added a short note in docs about suggested autoscaler thresholds.
Jordan Lee: Suggest adding a debug dump for tier occupancy and top-delta spans to help SRE during incidents.
Sofia Patel: Added a /debug/kv/tier-occupancy endpoint and top-delta spans to the existing debug dump (secured behind RBAC).
Aaron Chen: One nit: rename "cold" lane to "archival" to avoid confusion with 'cold start' semantics.
Sofia Patel: Renamed in allocator and docs (archival lane).
source.testing_and_validation:
- Unit tests for delta calculation and hysteresis edge cases.
- Integration test exercising warm rehydration path under contention.
- TSAN run to catch race in deferred coalesce (fixed).
- Perf-canary profile added and executed three times in staging.
- Manual validation: exercise streaming scenarios to confirm scheduler hints reduce kernel jitter.
source.docs_updated: RFC describing design and config knobs, metrics docs for pin_budget/pin_throttle_ratio/tier_occupancy, and a short howto for ops to interpret new metrics.
source.risk_and_mitigation: Risks: throttling could delay legitimate long-running pins causing extra load; mitigation: conservative defaults, runtime toggle, and metrics to observe throttle ratio. Deferred coalesce introduced a small window of fragmentation; mitigation: bounded epoch ages and opportunistic inline coalesces when low contention is detected.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash after approvals from reviewers and green CI. Changelog entry added and release notes updated for next patch release.

source.title: Safe Rejoin Coordinator with Connection Epoch Fence
source.author: Ava Chen
source.release_notes: Adds a coordinator to ensure safe node rejoin semantics with an epoch-based connection fence to prevent KV cache stomping during regional failover. Backportable to release/2024.11 as a hotfix.
source.description:
Motivation: During a cross-region incident we observed a small class of degradations where connections that rejoined quickly after transient network flaps caused KV cache overwrite and high tail latency spikes. This PR introduces a Safe Rejoin Coordinator and a Connection Epoch Fence to ensure rejoining nodes validate epoch affinity before accepting requests that may mutate or repopulate per-connection KV cache entries. The change reduces risk of stale-kv stomping and provides a short quiesce window for region rejoin sequences.

Scope: single runtime-side coordinator service + lightweight epoch fence protocol and control-plane signals for coordinated rejoin. The implementation intentionally keeps the runtime fast-path unchanged for steady state; the coordinator only participates during node state transitions.

What this PR changes: 1) runtime/rejoin/coordinator: new coordinator that tracks node epochs and issues timeboxed rejoin permits; 2) runtime/kv/fence: epoch-checks on KV-preserve paths to reject/forward ambiguous rejoin traffic; 3) controlplane/handlers: small handshake to annotate node reprovision events with epoch tokens; 4) ops tooling: a console toggle to enable conservative quiesce windows during rollouts.

Testing and validation: extensive unit tests for epoch validation logic, integration test that simulates multi-region reconnects with packet loss and ensures no KV overwrite, and a perf soak showing <1% p50 impact in steady-state. CI includes a canary job that runs the rejoin scenario harness.

Rollout plan: feature-gate off by default on main; staged rollout to production via the console toggle. Initial rollout will target non-critical regions (eu-west-2, ap-south-1) for 48 hours, then gradual expansion with telemetry checks for KV-inconsistency metrics. Runbook included for emergency rollback.

Checklist: [x] Unit tests added; [x] Integration scenario added to perf lab; [x] Console toggle implemented; [x] Release notes drafted.

Notes: This is designed as a low-risk path to reduce a very specific class of tail latency incidents tied to rejoin races. It is not intended to replace longer-term topology upgrades but mitigates immediate customer impact and enables safer hot restarts across regions.
commits: a1f9c3d: introduce safe rejoin coordinator scaffolding and interfaces
b34e2a1: implement epoch fence checks in KV preserve fastpath
c4d5f56: add control-plane epoch token propagation and API
d7a8b90: console toggle + CLI to query coordinator state
e9b1c22: integration tests for rejoin scenarios and perf soak entries
f02d3aa: docs + runbook for rollout and rollback procedures
review_comments: Lena Rodriguez: This looks sensible overall; two concerns: 1) can the epoch token leak across tenant boundaries? 2) is the quiesce window observable via existing metrics? Please add explicit assertions to the integration test to ensure tokens are never accepted from unrelated tenants.

Ava Chen (author): Good catches. Epoch tokens are scoped to node IDs + cluster namespace; added assertions in integration tests and a new metric 'rejoin.epoch_rejects_total' to trace rejected attempts. Also added a note in runbook about verifying token scoping.

Samir Patel: Implementation is fine but I want to see the perf numbers for 95/99 percentiles. The PR shows p50 is flat, but tail matters here. Can you add a short load test focused on p99 with small rejoin churn?

Ava Chen: Added a targeted p99 load test in perf-lab that simulates 2% rejoin churn; p99 tail increased by 3.1% in worst-case but remained below our SLO; documented in PR description and perf harness outputs.
Jordan Li: Approve after CI green and a brief canary in eu-west-2. Please coordinate the rollout with on-call; add a pre- and post-canary snapshot to the runbook.
Lena Rodriguez: Verified canary metrics for eu-west-2, no KV inconsistency observed, approving to merge.
post_merge_actions: Enable console toggle in eu-west-2 (canary) at 2024-11-13T02:00Z
If any KV inconsistency observed, rollback via helm chart rollback to previous release
Backport to release/2024.11 branch and cut hotfix build
notes: This PR targets a narrow rejoin-induced KV stomping bug and provides a conservative mitigation that can be enabled per-region.
Telemetry was added but low-cardinality to avoid signal explosion; ops can opt into verbose logging during diagnostics.
Further work: consider integrating coordinator decisions into admission controller for smarter warming; tracked in ENG-2051.

source.author: Priya Nair
source.release_notes: Introduces pausable/resumable stream lifecycle, cross-SDK credential handoff (credential-fuse), exposes retry-decision telemetry and new retry preset profiles. Minor SDK version bumps: py 1.22.0 -> 1.23.0, ts 2.9.0 -> 2.10.0, go 1.8.3 -> 1.9.0.
source.description: Summary: This PR implements a cross-language ergonomic surface for pausable/resumable streaming consumers, and a lightweight credential handoff mechanism we call 'credential-fuse' that reduces reauth churn during short-lived auth windows. Motivation: several teams reported latency spikes and redundant reauth calls during bursty streaming workloads; debugging showed frequent credential refresh races and lack of a clean pause/resume lifecycle on client streams. Goals: 1) provide pausable stream primitives that preserve local cursor/offset state, allow explicit credential handoff with minimal RPC, and provide telemetry for retry decisioning; 2) harmonize ergonomic APIs across Python/TypeScript/Go SDKs so examples and migration paths are consistent; 3) surface retry hints and a small set of typed retry presets to make opt-in behavior explicit. What changed (high level): - SDK Python: added StreamController.pause()/resume(), StreamContext.attach_credential_fuse(), small refactor of session manager to support short-lived fuse leases, new RetryPreset enum and telemetry labels (retry.reason, retry.hint). - SDK TypeScript: introduced StreamHandle.pause()/resume(), exported types for CredentialFuse and RetryPresets; simplified backoff constructor to accept named presets. - SDK Go: added stream.PausableStreamer interface, CredFuse struct and AttachFuse helper; exported RetryPreset constants and telemetry tags. - Core API compatibility: extended transport handshake to optionally carry a short-lived fuse token and a resume cursor; compatible fallback maintains old handshake shape. - Telemetry/CI: added new retry decision metrics (sdk.retry.decisions), fuse churn counters (sdk.auth.fuse_attempts), and stream lifecycle spans. - Docs/examples: updated tri-sdk examples repository with pause/resume demo and recommended retry presets for conversational and low-latency workloads. - Tests: added unit and integration tests for pause/resume semantics, credential transfer safety, and telemetry emission. Backwards compatibility: default behavior unchanged; fuse usage is opt-in. If server does not support fuse header, SDKs fall back to standard refresh flow. Migration notes: see README updates in SDK folders — primary change for integrators is replacing manual reconnect logic with StreamController.pause/resume when a transient auth flow is desired. Security notes: fuse tokens are single-use bearer tokens with TTL <= 30s and are logged only as hashes in telemetry. Checklist: - [x] API design review with platform (ENG-2419) - [x] Unit tests for Python/TS/Go - [x] Integration e2e against staging runtime - [x] Telemetry dashboards updated - [x] Docs + examples updated - [x] Version bumps and changelogs updated Performance: soldering fuse reduced reauth RPCs by ~67% in our internal canary (conversational agent workload) and improved median p95 reconnect latency by ~18ms in burst tests. source.title: introduce-route-auth-hint-header-and-compat-adapter-for-proxied-streaming-tool-calls
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Motivation: streaming tool calls and chained function/tool workflows suffer from fragile header semantics when requests are forwarded through edge proxies and gateway adapters (CORS preflight and streaming handshake especially). This PR adds a lightweight, opt-in per-route hint header (X-RW-Route-Auth) and a small compatibility adapter for OpenAI-compat endpoints so downstream proxies can safely forward scoped auth tokens and a short-lived signing bridge header. Design: 1) X-RW-Route-Auth (value: hint tokens or route id) allows the API gateway to advertise per-route auth expectations to downstream workers without exposing project keys. 2) RW-Signed-Bridge is an asymmetric-signed envelope (opaque string) the gateway can attach when a request needs to be proxied to an external tool runtime; workers validate the signature against a rotation-aware public key store. 3) Backwards compatible fallback: if neither header is present we keep existing project/org key semantics. 4) CORS: preflight responses are extended to include the new headers in Access-Control-Expose-Headers and Access-Control-Allow-Headers where appropriate. Implementation notes: server/proxy handles hint parsing and short-circuits token forwarding only for routes flagged in route config; a new compat layer in api/openai adapts X-RW-Route-Auth to the legacy X-Project header for older SDKs. Testing and rollout: feature-gated behind "route-auth-hint" feature flag (on by default for dedicated infra), unit+integration tests added, and manual canary in perf-canary for 48h before global merge. See linked Linear tickets for edge-case acceptance criteria.
commits: feat(proxy): add hint header parsing and validation for X-RW-Route-Auth
feat(openai-compat): map route hints to legacy X-Project header when compat mode enabled
feat(security): implement RW-Signed-Bridge validation and public key rotation consumer
docs: describe new headers and CORS implications in docs/auth.md
test: add integration test for streaming tool call passthrough with signed bridge
review_comments: Evan Morales: We should confirm the performance impact on hot path—parsing & verifying RW-Signed-Bridge must not allocate heavily during streaming. Can we move validation to a lightweight fast-path?

Priya Natarajan: Good point—validator uses a fast header parse and async signature verification pipeline for the initial handshake; streaming frames proceed before full verification if hint header is present, with a short TTL to allow fallback. Added bench numbers in PR description.

Priya Natarajan: Updated docs/auth.md with explicit example curl + browser preflight snippets and note about restricting expose-list for public routes.

source.title: stabilize-streaming-handshake-and-tool-handoff-with-role-serializer
source.author: Aisha Patel
source.description: Motivation: production incidents and internal fuzzing uncovered multiple edge cases in the /v1/chat.completions compatibility layer during streaming: (1) mixed stop tokens and framed JSON tool outputs would cause stream boundary misalignment, (2) role aliasing (user/assistant/function/tool) metadata was being lost during the stream->finalize transition leading to incorrect attribution in logs/metrics, and (3) logprob fallbacks were omitted when structured responses were negotiated in framed-json mode. This PR introduces a lightweight role serializer and a deterministic stop normalization pipeline to achieve parity with OpenAI /v1 semantics while preserving Redwood streaming guarantees. Summary of changes and rationale: 1) role serializer (api/compat/role_serializer.py): canonicalizes incoming message roles and preserves arbitrary role metadata in a compact JSON envelope attached to streaming chunks. This lets the runtime rehydrate message attribution at finalize time without blocking on full payloads. 2) stop normalization pipeline (api/compat/stop_normalizer.py): accepts OpenAI-style stop variants (string, array, regex-like tokens) and produces a prioritized sentinel sequence plus a short-circuit predicate used by the stream coordinator to detect boundaries deterministically. Normalizer also records original stop semantics for telemetry. 3) stream handshake extension (runtime/stream/handshake.cpp / handshake.py): adds a 3-way handshake for framed-json/toolcall streams that negotiates "framed-json-v1" capability and provides an escape-mode marker so tool outputs that embed stop-like tokens do not prematurely terminate the stream. 4) logprob fallback and structured-output adapter (api/compat/response_shaper.py): when clients request framed-json or structured output, the adapter will include best-effort token-level logprob summaries and a low-cost fallback that avoids invoking the heavy scorer when latency SLOs are tight. 5) SDK mappings (sdk/python/redwood/openai_compat.py and sdk/typescript/src/compat.ts): add role hints and an opt-in param "normalize_stops" to mirror parity behavior. 6) tests and harness: comprehensive unit tests and a new integration scenario under tests/integration/compat_streaming_tool_handoff.test that reproduces the incident condition. Implementation notes: - Conservative defaults: role serialization is enabled by default in the compatibility adapter but the opt-out flag (X-Redwood-Compat-Role-Serialize) is available for debugging. - Backwards compatibility: if a non-parity client doesn't set streaming frames, behavior falls back to the previous path. No existing stable API is removed. - Performance: microbenchmarks show ~3% median token latency overhead in worst-case framed-json negotiation; batching improvements in the runtime reclaim ~70% of this cost in steady-state. - Observability: added two new metrics: compat.role_serializer.malformed and compat.stop_normalizer.rewrites to detect malformed inputs and normalization frequency. Rollout plan: staged canary on dedicated clusters for 48 hours with traffic shaping (10%->30%->100%) and a kill-switch feature flag. If compat.role_serializer.malformed > 0.5% in canary traffic, the rollout will automatically roll back. Testing: added unit tests for role alias edge cases, stop normalization coverage for mixed stop arrays + tool content, and an end-to-end scenario that runs the framed-json handshake with toolcall resume. Also ran load tests against Dedicated with 1k concurrent streams to verify KV cache and batching interactions. Migration notes: clients using undocumented stop heuristics that relied on raw token echoes may see semantic difference when normalize_stops=true; recommended to opt-in and validate test prompts. See release_notes.
Commits in this PR: implement role serializer and preserve role metadata in stream envelope
add deterministic stop normalizer and predicates
extend handshake to negotiate framed-json toolcall escape mode
structured output adapter: logprob fallback and shaping
SDK: expose role_hints and normalize_stops flags
tests: unit + integration reproducer and load harness
docs: compatibility notes and rollout guide
Review conversation (high level): Marco Li (reviewer): "This is a solid approach. Two asks: add more unit coverage for nested tool outputs that contain stop tokens, and can you include a perf microbenchmark in the PR results?"
Author (Aisha): "Added nested tool output unit tests and included the canary microbench numbers in the PR description and test artifacts; see tests/perf/bench_framed_json.md in the commit."
Priya N. (security reviewer): "Ensure the serialized role envelope cannot be exploited for injection into logs/metrics. Prefer base64-encoded validated payloads for arbitrary metadata."
Author: "Switched envelope to base64 JSON and added a validation schema; updated tests."
Jonah E. (approver): "LGTM after one nit on variable naming; verified integration reproducer on my local env."

source.title: tool-annotations-stable-choice-serialization-and-fragment-safe-streaming
source.author: Maya Ortega
source.description: Context: Customers reported rare nondeterministic tool selection during streamed OpenAI-compatible responses where the model returned multiple candidate tool choices split across streaming fragments. This caused downstream systems to sometimes pick different tools after reassembly. Motivation: make the OpenAI adapter produce deterministic, replay-safe tool_choice selection and deliver tool payloads with fragment-safe markers so reassembly cannot change choice. What this PR does: 1) Introduces a canonical serialization path for tool choice and tool descriptors: ToolDescriptor v2 with canonical field ordering and stable hashing. This prevents dict-order nondeterminism when computing tool signatures used by routable policies. 2) Adds lightweight per-chunk framing markers in the streaming envelope to ensure the boundary between a tool_choice event and its associated tool payload cannot be split by arbitrary chunking. The runtime now inserts an explicit "chunk_boundary_hint" token in the stream for cases where a tool_choice is immediately followed by a tool payload. 3) Augments the adapter mapping rules so vendor tool schemas (OpenAPI-like or freeform) are normalized into ToolDescriptor v2, including explicit parameter typing and a normalized examples array. 4) Implements deterministic tie-breaking heuristics for tool_choice when the model returns multiple candidates with equal score: prefer higher-explicit-priority annotation, then lexicographic tool-id, then stable-hash of canonical descriptor. 5) Adds compatibility layer to avoid changing external OpenAI-compatible JSON for customers relying on legacy frames: the adapter emits the new markers only when client indicates support via the X-Redwood-OpenAI-Compat header. 6) Tests + benchmarks: unit tests for serialization round-trip, end-to-end streaming tests that simulate fragment boundaries, and a lightweight perf benchmark demonstrating ~1% median token-latency regression on small cases but eliminating nondeterministic reassembly failures. Checklist: - [x] Spec: include canonicalization rules and framing E2E contract - [x] Implementation: adapter + runtime framing + policy tie-breaker - [x] Tests: serializer unit tests, streaming e2e harness, and regression alerts - [x] Docs: SDK typings update and migration notes - [x] Rollout plan: feature-flagged by default off, opt-in via header; staged rollout to dedicated customers Impact/risk: low functional change (opt-in), but careful rollout required for clients that parse raw stream fragments. Related: ENG-4871 (deterministic routing), ENG-4920 (stream reassembly incidents), SUP-1221 (support ticket about changing tool in flight).

review_conversation:
Ivy Chen: Can you clarify why we need canonical JSON vs. protobuf? The team is concerned about adding yet another schema.
Maya Ortega: We considered protobuf but the OpenAI surface expects JSON and many clients reserialize; canonical JSON lets us interoperate without changing external shapes and avoids introducing a new wire format.

Lars Müller: The chunk_boundary_hint looks fine but will it increase stream size noticeably?
Maya Ortega: It adds a very small token in the stream only for boundary cases; benchmark shows <1% median token latency impact and negligible bandwidth increase on typical traffic.

Priya Natarajan: We should gate this behind the compat header for the first rollout to avoid surprising customers.
Maya Ortega: Agreed. Default behavior remains unchanged; header opt-in flips the adapter to emit framing markers and canonical descriptors.

Ivy Chen: Please add a real-world test using the mobile client chunker; it's been flaky before.
Maya Ortega: Added a mobile chunker simulation in the integration harness and ran it against two deduped agent scenarios; results included in CI.
source.release_notes: Adds an opt-in OpenAI adapter feature that emits canonical tool descriptors and fragment-safe streaming markers to eliminate nondeterministic tool selection during fragmented streaming. Backwards-compatible and gated by X-Redwood-OpenAI-Compat header.
source.notes_for_releasers: Feature is off by default. To enable for a customer, flip runtime feature flag 'openai_adapter.canonical_tool_choice' and instruct client to set X-Redwood-OpenAI-Compat: v2. Monitor streaming_reassembly_failures alert for first 48h.
source.migration_steps: 1) Clients that parse raw framed fragments must opt-in after testing; 2) SDKs updated with ToolDescriptor v2 typedefs; 3) Operators can enable feature-flag per-customer in Console; 4) If issues, revert by disabling header or rolling back feature flag.
source.notes: This PR intentionally minimizes surface area change by keeping external JSON stable unless the compat header is present. It centralizes tool normalization logic to make future policy work easier.
